question: In which year did the Berlin Wall fall?
context: Fell on 9 November after an East German spokesman misread new travel regulations at a press conference; crowds surged to the checkpoints that night. Formal German reunification followed on 3 October 1990.

question: Who was the first emperor of Rome?
context: Born Gaius Octavius, adoptive heir of Julius Caesar. Took the title 'Augustus' in 27 BCE after defeating Mark Antony and Cleopatra at Actium (31 BCE). His reign initiated the Pax Romana.

question: The Hundred Years' War was fought between which two kingdoms?
context: Actually lasted 116 years (1337–1453), fought over English claims to the French throne. Joan of Arc catalysed the French recovery. The English longbow revolutionised warfare at Crécy and Agincourt.

question: Which treaty ended World War I?
context: Signed on 28 June 1919 — exactly five years after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand. The 'war guilt' clause and reparations on Germany are widely cited as contributing causes of WWII.

question: Who was the longest-reigning monarch in British history?
context: Reigned 1952–2022. Surpassed Queen Victoria's record in 2015. Succeeded by her son Charles III.

question: In what year was the Dutch East India Company (VOC) dissolved?
context: Founded in 1602 as the world's first joint-stock company and first multinational. Collapsed under debt, corruption and the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War; its debts were absorbed by the Dutch state.

question: Which 1815 battle ended Napoleon's rule?
context: On 18 June 1815 in present-day Belgium. A British-led coalition under Wellington, reinforced by Blücher's Prussians, defeated Napoleon. He was exiled to Saint Helena, where he died in 1821.

question: The Ottoman Empire officially ended in which year?
context: The Sultanate was abolished in November 1922; the Turkish Republic was proclaimed on 29 October 1923. The empire had lasted roughly 624 years from its founding around 1299.

question: Who painted "The Night Watch" (De Nachtwacht)?
context: Officially 'Militia Company of District II under the Command of Captain Frans Banninck Cocq' (1642). Its dark appearance comes from old varnish and grime — it was originally a daytime scene.

question: In which year did India gain independence from Britain?
context: 15 August 1947. Accompanied by the Partition creating Pakistan, which displaced around 15 million people and killed an estimated 1–2 million in the ensuing violence.

question: Who was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize?
context: Polish-French physicist and chemist. Still the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences (Physics 1903, Chemistry 1911). Died in 1934 of aplastic anaemia, almost certainly from radiation exposure.

question: The Great Fire of London occurred in which year?
context: 2–6 September 1666. Started in a bakery on Pudding Lane. Destroyed around 13,200 houses but officially killed only six (true toll likely higher). Led to modern building codes and Wren's St Paul's.

question: Which Roman emperor converted to Christianity?
context: Converted after the Battle of the Milvian Bridge (312 CE). Issued the Edict of Milan (313) granting religious tolerance. Founded Constantinople in 330.

question: In which year did the Dutch formally declare independence from Spain (Plakkaat van Verlatinghe)?
context: The Act of Abjuration, 26 July 1581. Formally deposed Philip II of Spain. Only recognised internationally after the Peace of Westphalia (1648). Some historians argue it influenced the US Declaration of Independence.

question: The Byzantine Empire fell when Constantinople was captured in which year?
context: 29 May 1453. Sultan Mehmed II the Conqueror used massive cannons (some up to 8m long) to breach the Theodosian walls, which had held for a thousand years. Often cited as the end of the Middle Ages.

question: In which year did the French Revolution begin?
context: Storming of the Bastille, 14 July 1789 (still the French national holiday). Led to the execution of Louis XVI in 1793 and the Reign of Terror (1793–94).

question: Which ship sank on 15 April 1912?
context: Struck an iceberg at 23:40 on 14 April and sank at around 02:20 on 15 April 1912. About 1,500 of some 2,240 people on board died. Wreck found by Robert Ballard in 1985 at 3,800m depth.

question: Who led the Soviet Union during World War II?
context: Led the USSR from Lenin's death (1924) until his own in 1953. Responsible for mass purges, the gulag system, and engineered famines (including the Holodomor) that killed millions.

question: The Magna Carta was signed in which year?
context: Signed by King John at Runnymede, 15 June 1215. Established that the king was subject to law. Only three of its 63 clauses remain in English law today.

question: Who was the last Tsar of Russia?
context: Abdicated in March 1917 during the February Revolution. He and his immediate family were executed by Bolsheviks in Yekaterinburg on 17 July 1918.

question: Which explorer first reached the South Pole?
context: Reached the Pole on 14 December 1911, beating British rival Robert Falcon Scott by 34 days. Scott and his team died on the return. Amundsen used skis and sled dogs; Scott relied partly on ponies.

question: The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred in which year?
context: 16–28 October 1962. Closest the world has come to nuclear war. Resolved when the Soviets withdrew missiles from Cuba in exchange for a public US pledge not to invade and a secret removal of US missiles from Turkey.

question: Who was the first Chancellor of unified Germany in 1871?
context: Architect of German unification via three wars (1864, 1866, 1870–71). Known for Realpolitik and for creating the world's first modern welfare state. Dismissed by Kaiser Wilhelm II in 1890.

question: In which year did the Netherlands host the Winter Olympics?
answer: Never — the Netherlands has never hosted the Winter Olympics (they did host the 1928 Summer Games in Amsterdam).
context: The Netherlands is a speed-skating powerhouse yet has never bid successfully for a Winter Games. Amsterdam hosted the 1928 Summer Olympics — the first time a lit Olympic flame was used.

question: Which conflict ended with the Peace of Westphalia in 1648?
answer: The Thirty Years' War (and for the Dutch, the Eighty Years' War)
context: Two treaties signed at Osnabrück and Münster in 1648. Established the modern concept of state sovereignty ('Westphalian sovereignty'). For the Dutch it also ended the Eighty Years' War for independence.
option_d: The War of the Spanish Succession

question: What is the capital of Australia?
context: Purpose-built capital chosen in 1908 as a compromise between rival claims of Sydney and Melbourne. Designed by Chicago architect Walter Burley Griffin.

question: Mount Kilimanjaro is located in which country?
context: At 5,895m, the highest mountain in Africa and the world's tallest free-standing mountain. A dormant stratovolcano. Its summit glaciers have lost over 80% of their mass since 1912.

question: What is the smallest country in the world?
context: 0.49 km², about 800 residents. Enclave within Rome, established as a sovereign state by the Lateran Treaty of 1929.

question: Which strait separates Europe from Africa?
context: About 14 km wide at its narrowest. Named after the Arabic 'Jabal Tariq' (Mountain of Tariq). Spain has long disputed UK sovereignty over the Rock itself.

question: How many countries border Germany?
answer: 9 (Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, France, Switzerland, Austria, Czech Republic, Poland)
context: Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, France, Switzerland, Austria, Czech Republic, Poland. Tied with France (8 in Europe plus overseas neighbours) for most borders in Western Europe.

question: What is the deepest point in the world's oceans?
context: About 10,935m deep. Pressure at the bottom is roughly 1,086 bar — over a thousand times atmospheric pressure. Only three crewed descents have ever been made.

question: The Danube flows through more countries than any other river — how many?
context: Germany, Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, Ukraine. Second-longest river in Europe after the Volga.

question: Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
context: Around 202,080 km including thousands of Arctic islands — over six times longer than the second-place country (Indonesia or Norway, depending on methodology).

question: The Dead Sea borders which two countries?
context: Also borders the West Bank. Lowest point on Earth's land surface (around -430m and dropping). Salinity is about 34% — roughly ten times that of ocean water.

question: Which country has the most time zones (including overseas territories)?
context: From French Polynesia (UTC-10) to Wallis and Futuna (UTC+12). Mainland France alone spans only one time zone.

question: The Pyrenees form the natural border between which two countries?
context: About 491 km long. Also borders the microstate of Andorra. Geologically much older than the Alps (formed around 55 million years ago).

question: What is the capital of Canada?
context: Chosen by Queen Victoria in 1857 as a compromise between French- and English-speaking rivals Quebec City, Montreal, Kingston and Toronto.

question: What is the highest waterfall in the world?
context: In Canaima National Park. Named after American aviator Jimmie Angel, who crashed nearby in 1937. Venezuela officially renamed it Kerepakupai Merú in 2009.

question: Lake Baikal — the world's deepest lake — is in which country?
context: About 1,642m deep. Also the world's oldest lake (25–30 million years) and holds roughly 20% of Earth's unfrozen freshwater. Home to the nerpa, the only exclusively freshwater seal.

question: What is the only sea on Earth without coasts?
answer: The Sargasso Sea (bounded by ocean currents, not land)
context: In the North Atlantic, bounded not by land but by four ocean currents. The unique breeding ground for European and American eels.

question: The Amazon rainforest spans multiple countries — which country contains the majority?
context: Roughly 60% of the Amazon rainforest is in Brazil. The rest spans Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, Guyana, Suriname and French Guiana.

question: Greenland is geographically part of which continent?
context: Politically a constituent country of the Kingdom of Denmark; geographically part of North America. World's largest island (Australia being classified as a continent).

question: What is the highest point in the Netherlands?
context: At the Drielandenpunt — the tripoint of Netherlands, Belgium and Germany. Mount Scenery on Saba (887m) is technically the highest point in the full Kingdom of the Netherlands.

question: Which country has the most islands?
context: Most are tiny granite skerries. Finland is close behind with around 188,000. The count depends heavily on minimum-size criteria.

question: The Strait of Malacca lies between which two major landmasses?
answer: The Malay Peninsula and the island of Sumatra
context: Between the Malay Peninsula and Sumatra. One of the world's most important shipping lanes — roughly 25% of global trade and 40% of oil trade pass through it.

question: What is the capital of South Korea?
context: Metropolitan area of about 25 million — roughly half of South Korea's entire population.

question: Which three countries in the world are entirely landlocked and surrounded by only one other country?
answer: Lesotho (surrounded by South Africa), Vatican City, and San Marino (both surrounded by Italy)
context: All three are full enclave states. Lesotho, Vatican City and San Marino are the only sovereign states completely surrounded by a single other country.
option_d: Lesotho, Vatican City, and San Marino

question: The Atacama Desert is in which country?
context: Driest non-polar desert in the world. Some weather stations have never recorded rainfall. Used as a Mars analogue for testing rovers.

question: Which African country was never formally colonized by a European power?
context: Defeated Italy at the Battle of Adwa in 1896. Italy did occupy Ethiopia from 1936–41 under Mussolini, but this is usually not counted as formal colonisation. Liberia is also sometimes cited.

question: What is the capital of New Zealand?
context: Southernmost capital of any sovereign state. Chosen in 1865 partly for its central location between North and South Islands.

question: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
context: From Latin 'aurum', meaning shining dawn. Element 79. Valued for around 6,000 years; never rusts or tarnishes.

question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
context: Adults have 206. Babies are born with about 270; many fuse during development. Over half of all adult bones are in the hands and feet.

question: What is the fastest land animal?
context: Top speed around 100–112 km/h. Accelerates 0–100 km/h faster than most supercars. The only cat species with non-retractable claws — essentially running shoes.

question: Which planet in our solar system has the most moons?
answer: Saturn (292 confirmed as of April 2026 — the count keeps rising as new surveys find small irregular moons; Saturn overtook Jupiter in 2023)
context: The 2023 surveys put Saturn ahead of Jupiter for the first time. Almost all newly discovered moons are small irregular 'shepherd' moons, not round bodies.

question: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
context: About 78.09%. Inert under normal conditions. Discovered by Daniel Rutherford in 1772.

question: How many elements are currently on the periodic table?
context: Complete through oganesson (Og, element 118). The last four (113, 115, 117, 118) were officially added in 2016. All elements beyond uranium (92) are essentially synthetic.

question: What is the hardest naturally occurring substance on Earth?
context: Scores 10 on the Mohs scale. Pure carbon crystallised under extreme pressure. Lab-grown diamonds are now chemically identical but traceable via trace-element signatures.

question: Who formulated the general theory of relativity?
context: Published in 1915; special relativity came earlier in 1905. Predicted gravitational time dilation, black holes and gravitational waves (first directly detected in 2015).

question: What does DNA stand for?
context: The double-helix structure was published by Watson and Crick in 1953, relying on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography without proper credit. Every nucleated human cell contains around 2m of DNA.

question: What is the largest organ in the human body?
context: About 1.5–2 m² of surface area and 3–5 kg in adults. Regenerates completely roughly every 27 days.

question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
context: Two branchial hearts pump blood through the gills; one systemic heart pumps it to the body. Their blood is copper-based (haemocyanin), which makes it blue.

question: Which blood type is the universal donor?
context: Lacks A, B and Rh antigens. Only about 7% of people have it. The universal plasma donor is AB (plasma contains no A or B antibodies).

question: What is the pH of pure water?
context: Neutral at 25°C. Actual rainwater is typically around 5.6 due to dissolved CO₂; 'acid rain' is usually defined as below pH 5.

question: Who discovered penicillin, and in what year?
context: Fleming noticed a mould (Penicillium notatum) had killed bacteria on a petri dish he left out during a holiday. Development into a usable drug came from Florey and Chain in 1940–42; all three shared the Nobel in 1945.

question: What is the largest planet in our solar system?
context: About 11 Earth diameters wide; contains more than twice the mass of all other planets combined. The Great Red Spot has been raging for at least 350 years.

question: How many chromosomes do humans have?
context: 22 autosome pairs plus one sex chromosome pair (XX or XY). Chimpanzees have 48 — a fusion event created human chromosome 2.

question: Which scientist formulated the three laws of motion?
context: Published in 'Principia' (1687). Also co-invented calculus, simultaneously with and independently of Leibniz, triggering a bitter priority dispute.

question: What is the name of our galaxy?
context: Barred spiral galaxy, about 100,000 light-years across, containing 100–400 billion stars. Named for its milky appearance in the night sky — 'galaxias' in Greek means milky.

question: What is photosynthesis?
answer: The process by which plants use sunlight to convert water and CO₂ into energy and oxygen
context: Simplified: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Chlorophyll captures energy from photons. Responsible for essentially all atmospheric oxygen.
option_a: Plants convert sunlight, water and CO₂ into energy and oxygen
option_b: Plants convert CO₂ directly into glucose without water
option_c: Plants absorb nitrogen from the soil to build proteins
option_d: Plants break down starch to release stored energy

question: What is the study of earthquakes called?
context: From Greek 'seismos' (earthquake) plus 'logia' (study). The modern field began in the late 19th century with the invention of the seismograph.

question: What is the lightest element on the periodic table?
context: Makes up about 75% of the universe's normal matter. One proton, one electron, no neutron in its most common isotope.

question: What's the second most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
context: Enough to support combustion but not so much that fires become uncontrollable. Essentially all of it is produced by plants and cyanobacteria via photosynthesis.

question: What is the speed of light in a vacuum (approximately)?
context: Exactly 299,792,458 m/s by definition — the metre itself is now defined in terms of this constant. The fundamental speed limit for information and matter.

question: What does the "H" in H₂O stand for?
context: Two hydrogen atoms plus one oxygen. Water is unusual for expanding when it freezes, which is why ice floats.

question: Which mammal is the only one capable of sustained flight?
context: Sustained, powered flight — not just gliding like flying squirrels or colugos. Around 1,400 species, roughly 20% of all mammal species.

question: Who wrote "One Hundred Years of Solitude"?
context: Published 1967. Defining work of magical realism. Márquez, Colombian, won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982.

question: Who wrote "The Divine Comedy"?
context: Written around 1308–1321 in Italian rather than Latin — a radical choice that helped shape the Italian language. Three parts: Inferno, Purgatorio, Paradiso.

question: In which novel does Holden Caulfield appear?
answer: "The Catcher in the Rye" by J.D. Salinger
context: Published 1951. Made 'phony' a cultural term. Salinger became famously reclusive after the novel's success.

question: Who wrote "Crime and Punishment"?
context: Published 1866. A psychological exploration of guilt via Raskolnikov, a poor student who murders a pawnbroker to test a theory about 'extraordinary men'.

question: Who wrote "1984"?
context: Published 1949, written during Orwell's terminal illness. Coined 'Big Brother', 'doublethink', 'thoughtcrime' and 'Newspeak'. The title inverts 1948, the year he completed it.

question: Which Dutch author wrote "De Aanslag" (The Assault)?
context: Published 1982. Follows a WWII reprisal killing in Haarlem and its long shadow. Its film adaptation won the 1987 Best Foreign Language Oscar.

question: What is the longest novel in Western literature, commonly cited?
answer: "In Search of Lost Time" by Marcel Proust
context: Around 4,215 pages and 1.2 million words, across seven volumes (1913–1927). Famously opens with the madeleine scene triggering involuntary memory.

question: Who wrote "Pride and Prejudice"?
context: Published 1813. Original title was 'First Impressions'. Its opening line ('It is a truth universally acknowledged...') is one of the most recognised in English.

question: Who wrote "Don Quixote"?
context: Published in two parts, 1605 and 1615. Often called the first modern novel. Cervantes lost the use of his left hand at the Battle of Lepanto (1571).

question: Which poet wrote "The Waste Land"?
context: Published 1922, the annus mirabilis of modernism (also Joyce's Ulysses). Heavily edited by Ezra Pound. 434 lines of fragmented allusions.

question: Who wrote "The Name of the Rose"?
context: Published 1980 in Italian. A semiotics-infused medieval murder mystery set in a 14th-century abbey, by the professor-novelist Umberto Eco.

question: In which Shakespeare play does Lady Macbeth appear?
context: Shakespeare's shortest tragedy. Stage tradition forbids speaking its name in a theatre — it's referred to as 'the Scottish play'.

question: Who wrote "Beloved"?
context: Published 1987, Pulitzer Prize 1988. Based on the true story of Margaret Garner, an escaped slave who killed her daughter rather than let her be recaptured. Morrison was the first Black woman to win the Literature Nobel (1993).

question: Who is Agatha Christie's famous Belgian detective?
context: Christie's most famous detective after Miss Marple. Appeared in 33 novels. Christie grew to dislike him but kept writing him for commercial reasons.

question: Who wrote "Les Misérables"?
context: Published 1862. Contains a famously long single sentence of around 823 words. Set against the June Rebellion of 1832 — not the 1789 Revolution, a common misconception.

question: Who wrote "The Discovery of Heaven" (De Ontdekking van de Hemel)?
context: Published 1992. Voted the best Dutch novel of all time in a 2007 NRC Handelsblad readers' poll.

question: Who wrote "Frankenstein"?
context: Published 1818, when Shelley was 20. Written during the 'Year Without a Summer' (1816) at Lake Geneva — the same contest that produced Polidori's 'The Vampyre'. Often called the first science-fiction novel.

question: Which novel begins "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times"?
answer: "A Tale of Two Cities" by Charles Dickens
context: Published 1859, set during the French Revolution. Often cited as the best-selling novel of all time (estimates around 200 million copies).

question: Who wrote "War and Peace"?
context: Published 1867. About 587,000 words. Set during Napoleon's 1812 invasion of Russia. Tolstoy himself refused to call it a novel.

question: Who wrote "Norwegian Wood"?
context: Published 1987 in Japanese. Title borrowed from the Beatles song. The book that turned Murakami from a cult author into a mainstream phenomenon in Japan.

question: Who wrote "The Sorrows of Young Werther"?
context: Published 1774. Triggered 'Werther Fever' — young men dressed like the protagonist and there was a reported spate of copycat suicides. Made Goethe famous overnight.

question: Who wrote "Things Fall Apart"?
context: Published 1958. Nigerian. First major African novel in English to achieve international recognition. Explores Igbo society's encounter with British colonialism.

question: Who wrote the "Lord of the Rings" trilogy?
context: Published 1954–55. Tolkien was a professor of Anglo-Saxon at Oxford. Originally conceived as a sequel to The Hobbit; grew into a much larger mythology.

question: In which year was Anne Frank's diary first published?
context: Published by her father Otto, the only surviving member of the family, originally in Dutch as 'Het Achterhuis'. Anne died at Bergen-Belsen in early 1945, months before publication.

question: Who wrote "Brave New World"?
context: Published 1932. A dystopia of state-engineered happiness via soma, hypnopaedia and genetic castes. Often paired with 1984 as opposite visions of tyranny — Huxley's: we love our oppression; Orwell's: we fear it.

question: Which film won Best Picture at the 2020 Oscars?
context: First non-English-language film to win Best Picture. Also took Best Director, Original Screenplay and International Feature — a clean sweep.
option_a: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood

question: Who composed "The Four Seasons"?
context: Composed around 1720. A set of four violin concertos, each depicting a season, with accompanying sonnets likely written by Vivaldi himself.

question: Which band released the album "The Dark Side of the Moon"?
context: Stayed on the Billboard 200 for 741 consecutive weeks. Explores conflict, greed, time, death and mental illness.

question: Who directed "Pulp Fiction"?
context: Released 1994. Won the Palme d'Or and the Best Original Screenplay Oscar. Revived John Travolta's career and took non-linear storytelling mainstream.

question: In which year did The Beatles officially break up?
context: Paul McCartney publicly announced his departure in April 1970. The legal partnership was not formally dissolved until 1975.

question: Which composer wrote the "Ode to Joy" (used as the EU anthem)?
context: Completed in 1824 when Beethoven was entirely deaf. Incorporated Schiller's poem 'An die Freude'. First major symphony to use a chorus. Adopted as EU anthem in 1985.

question: Who directed "Schindler's List"?
context: Released 1993. Filmed in black and white in Kraków. Won seven Oscars including Best Picture and Director. Spielberg took no salary and donated his profits to the Shoah Foundation.

question: What is the name of the fictional African country in "Black Panther"?
context: A secretly technologically advanced African nation with vibranium resources. The 2018 film grossed roughly $1.3 billion.

question: Who composed "The Marriage of Figaro"?
context: Premiered 1786 in Vienna. Based on a Beaumarchais play banned in France for its portrayal of aristocratic abuse of power.

question: Who plays Michael Corleone in "The Godfather"?
context: Three films (1972, 1974, 1990). Pacino was an unknown stage actor when cast; Paramount executives wanted Robert Redford or Ryan O'Neal.

question: Which HBO series ended in 2019 amid backlash over its final season?
context: Eight seasons (2011–2019). Fans started a petition to remake the final season that gathered about 1.8 million signatures. Based on George R.R. Martin's still-unfinished novel series.

question: Who wrote the opera cycle "The Ring of the Nibelung"?
context: Four operas, roughly 15 hours in total. Premiered 1876 at Bayreuth. Popularised the leitmotif — the recurring musical-character association now used in virtually every film score.

question: Which Beatles album closes with "Her Majesty"?
context: Recorded in 1969. Last Beatles album actually recorded (Let It Be was released later but recorded earlier). Famous zebra-crossing cover.
option_d: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band

question: Who was the lead singer of Queen?
context: Born Farrokh Bulsara in Zanzibar, 1946. Died 1991 of AIDS-related complications. Bandmates: Brian May, Roger Taylor, John Deacon.

question: Who composed "The Rite of Spring"?
context: Premiered 29 May 1913 in Paris. Its pagan rhythms and dissonances caused a legendary riot at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées. Commissioned by Diaghilev for the Ballets Russes.

question: Which film features the line "Here's looking at you, kid"?
context: Released in 1942 while WWII was still on. Almost nobody involved thought it would become a classic — it was seen as just another studio picture.

question: Who created the musical "Hamilton"?
context: Premiered 2015. Cast the US Founding Fathers as people of colour, with a hip-hop and R&B score. Won 11 Tonys and a Pulitzer.

question: Which Netflix series is set in Hawkins, Indiana?
context: Netflix, from 2016. Created by the Duffer Brothers. Set in the 1980s; heavy homage to Spielberg, Stephen King and John Carpenter.

question: Who directed the "Lord of the Rings" film trilogy?
context: Three films 2001–2003. Shot simultaneously in New Zealand. 'The Return of the King' (2003) tied the record for most Oscars won by a single film (11).

question: What instrument did Louis Armstrong play?
context: Nicknamed 'Satchmo'. Born 1901 in New Orleans. A foundational figure in jazz, both as player and singer — also famous for 'What a Wonderful World'.

question: Which artist released the album "21" in 2011?
context: Best-selling album of the 21st century in the UK and US. Includes 'Rolling in the Deep' and 'Someone Like You'. Named for her age at the time of writing.

question: Who composed the "Star Wars" film score?
context: Also scored Jaws, ET, Indiana Jones, Jurassic Park, Harry Potter and Schindler's List. Has received around 54 Oscar nominations — the most of any living person.

question: In which year did the first Eurovision Song Contest take place?
context: Held in Lugano, Switzerland. Only seven countries participated. Switzerland won. Eurovision has grown into one of the world's most-watched non-sporting TV events (~160 million viewers).

question: Who directed "The Godfather"?
context: Adapted from Mario Puzo's 1969 novel. Coppola was nearly fired several times during production. The first two films won Best Picture.

question: Which band released "Hotel California" in 1976?
context: Title track co-written by Don Felder, Don Henley and Glenn Frey. Interpretations range from a critique of hedonism to a metaphor for the music industry.

question: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
context: Painted around 1503–1519. Subject most likely Lisa Gherardini, wife of Florentine merchant Francesco del Giocondo (hence its Italian name 'La Gioconda'). Stolen in 1911 by an Italian handyman — which is what made it globally famous.

question: In which museum is the Mona Lisa displayed?
context: Behind bulletproof glass in the Salle des États. World's most visited museum (8–9 million visitors/year). The painting is small (77 × 53 cm) and often underwhelms visitors expecting something monumental.

question: Which post-impressionist painter cut off part of his own ear?
context: Cut his left ear in December 1888 in Arles after a dispute with Gauguin. Sold only one painting during his lifetime. Died at 37 from a gunshot wound — traditionally ruled suicide, though debated.

question: Who painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?
context: Painted 1508–1512, commissioned by Pope Julius II. Michelangelo considered himself primarily a sculptor and resented the job. The famous 'Creation of Adam' is one panel out of nine.

question: Which art movement is Salvador Dalí most associated with?
context: Expelled from the Surrealist group in 1934 over political disputes. Known for his eccentric persona and waxed moustache. His wife Gala was also his business manager.

question: Who designed the Guggenheim Museum in New York?
context: NYC building opened in 1959, six months after Wright's death. The spiral ramp was highly controversial — and practical problems (tilted floors, curved walls) persist to this day.

question: Which Dutch painter created "Girl with a Pearl Earring"?
context: Dutch Golden Age painter from Delft. Only around 34 works are definitively attributed to him. 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' (~1665) is often called the 'Mona Lisa of the North'.

question: In which country did the Renaissance begin?
context: Began in the 14th century in Florence. Funded heavily by the Medici banking family. Spread across Europe by the 16th century.

question: Who painted "The Persistence of Memory" (the melting clocks)?
context: Painted 1931. Dalí said the idea came from watching Camembert cheese melt in the sun. Held at MoMA in New York.

question: Which Spanish painter co-founded Cubism with Georges Braque?
context: Developed around 1907–1914 with Georges Braque. Influenced by Iberian sculpture and African masks. 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' (1907) is usually cited as the proto-Cubist breakthrough.

question: Who painted "The Scream"?
context: Painted 1893. Four versions exist (two paintings, two pastels). Munch wrote that he painted it after feeling 'an infinite scream passing through nature'.

question: In which city is the Prado Museum?
context: Houses the Spanish royal collection. Highlights include Velázquez's 'Las Meninas', Goya's 'The Third of May 1808' and Bosch's work.

question: What does MoMA stand for?
answer: The Museum of Modern Art (New York)
context: Founded in 1929 — terrible timing in retrospect, just before the Great Depression. Houses Starry Night, Les Demoiselles d'Avignon and The Persistence of Memory.

question: Who commissioned the Taj Mahal, and for whom?
answer: Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan, for his wife Mumtaz Mahal
context: Built around 1632–1653. Made of white Makrana marble that subtly changes colour throughout the day. Inlaid with precious and semi-precious stones.

question: Which Picasso painting depicts the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War?
context: Painted 1937 in about five weeks for the Spanish Republic's pavilion at the Paris World's Fair. Monochrome, 3.49m × 7.77m. Returned to Spain from MoMA only after Franco's death in 1981 — now at the Reina Sofía in Madrid.

question: Piet Mondrian was associated with which Dutch art movement?
context: Founded 1917 by Mondrian and Theo van Doesburg. Strict grid of straight lines and primary colours. Heavily influenced modern graphic design and the Bauhaus.

question: Who sculpted the statue "David" in Florence?
context: 5.17m marble statue completed in 1504. Originally commissioned for Florence Cathedral; its size and quality led to placement in the Piazza della Signoria instead. Now in the Galleria dell'Accademia.

question: The Rijksmuseum is in which Dutch city?
context: Houses the national art and history collection. Home to the Night Watch, Vermeer's Milkmaid and much more. Reopened in 2013 after a ten-year, €375M renovation.

question: Which painting gave Impressionism its name?
context: The name was coined mockingly by critic Louis Leroy in 1874 — and the artists promptly adopted it.

question: Who painted "The Garden of Earthly Delights"?
context: Triptych, around 1490–1510. Left panel: paradise. Centre: a hallucinatory scene of human pleasure. Right: hellish punishment. In the Prado, Madrid.

question: Which architect designed the Sagrada Família in Barcelona?
context: Construction began 1882. Gaudí spent his final 43 years on it. Still unfinished; targeted for completion around 2026, the centenary of his death (he was hit by a tram in 1926).

question: What is the name of the armless marble Venus sculpture in the Louvre?
context: Discovered 1820 on the Greek island of Milos. Dated roughly 150–125 BCE. The original position of its missing arms remains debated.

question: Who painted the "Water Lilies" series?
context: Around 250 paintings made at his garden in Giverny over some 30 years. The largest examples are about 12m long. Much of the series was completed while Monet was suffering severe cataracts.

question: Which painting sold for around $450 million in 2017, making it the most expensive publicly sold painting?
answer: "Salvator Mundi", attributed to Leonardo da Vinci
context: Sold at Christie's New York in 2017. The buyer was later revealed as Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. Its attribution to Leonardo is still contested by some scholars.

question: Which Dutch graphic artist is famous for impossible constructions like the Penrose stairs?
context: Dutch, 1898–1972. Despite the strong mathematical flavour of his work, he had little formal training in maths — he collaborated with mathematicians like Roger Penrose, whose staircase inspired 'Ascending and Descending'.

question: How many players does a football (soccer) team field?
context: Including the goalkeeper. Standardised in the 1863 FA rules.

question: In which year did the modern Olympic Games begin?
context: Revived by Pierre de Coubertin in 1896 after a gap of roughly 1,500 years. 241 athletes from 14 countries competed — all men.

question: How many rings are on the Olympic flag?
context: Representing five continents (Europe, Africa, Americas, Asia, Oceania — the Americas treated as one). Designed by Coubertin in 1913. The colours were chosen so that every national flag would have at least one.

question: Which country won the first FIFA World Cup in 1930?
context: Hosted and won on home soil. The final against Argentina ended 4–2. Several European teams skipped the tournament due to the travel time and cost.

question: In which sport is the Ryder Cup contested?
context: Biennial team match between the US and Europe (originally Great Britain, expanded in 1979). Named after seed merchant Samuel Ryder.

question: How many players are on a cricket team?
context: Similar to football in numbers but radically different in structure. Standardised in the 18th century.

question: How long is a marathon?
context: The distance was fixed at the 1908 London Olympics — extended from about 40 km to exactly 42.195 km to stretch from Windsor Castle to the royal box at White City Stadium.

question: In which years did the Netherlands reach the men's World Cup final?
context: Lost to West Germany in 1974 (2–1), Argentina in 1978 (3–1), Spain in 2010 (1–0 after extra time). Often called the best team never to have won the World Cup.

question: Which club has won the most UEFA Champions League titles?
context: 15 titles (as of 2024), including five in a row under the old European Cup format (1956–60) and dominant again in the modern era.

question: Who is the only boxer in history widely called "The Greatest"?
context: Born Cassius Clay. Three-time heavyweight champion. Refused induction into the Vietnam War in 1967, was stripped of his title and barred for around three years. Died 2016.

question: In tennis, what is the term for a score of 40-40?
context: From Old French 'à deux' (at two) — meaning two points still needed to win. Player must win by two.

question: Which Grand Slam tournament is played on grass?
context: Oldest Grand Slam (founded 1877). The only Slam still played on grass, with a strict all-white dress code.

question: How many points is a try worth in rugby union?
context: Originally worth zero — the try literally just earned you the chance to 'try' a conversion kick. Raised to 5 in 1992 to incentivise attacking play.

question: Which country is credited with inventing ice hockey?
context: First organised indoor game: Montreal, 1875. Rules codified at McGill University. Legally the national winter sport of Canada.

question: In golf, what is a "birdie"?
context: Eagle = 2 under, albatross/double eagle = 3 under, bogey = 1 over. 'Birdie' comes from early 20th-century American slang where 'bird' meant excellent.

question: The Vuelta a España is a cycling race in which country?
context: One of cycling's three Grand Tours, alongside the Tour de France and the Giro d'Italia. Held each September.

question: In tennis, what word means "zero"?
context: Probably from French 'l'oeuf' (egg, zero-shaped) — though the etymology is debated.

question: Who holds the record for most goals scored at a single men's FIFA World Cup?
context: 13 goals in six games, at the 1958 World Cup in Sweden. A record unlikely to fall given modern tournament structure and squad rotation.

question: Which NBA player won 6 championships with the Chicago Bulls wearing number 23?
context: Won titles in 1991, 1992, 1993, 1996, 1997 and 1998 — two 'three-peats'. Widely considered the greatest basketball player ever.

question: In which city were the 2024 Summer Olympics held?
context: Paris's third Olympics (1900, 1924, 2024). Opening ceremony along the Seine rather than in a stadium — the first of its kind.

question: Which Dutch football club has won the most Eredivisie titles?
context: 36 titles. Training academy has produced Cruyff, Van Basten, Bergkamp, De Jong, De Ligt and many others.

question: How many Formula 1 World Championships has Lewis Hamilton won?
context: 2008, 2014, 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020. Also holds F1 records for most wins and most pole positions.

question: In which sport is the Stanley Cup awarded?
context: Oldest trophy in North American professional sport. Awarded annually since 1893. Every winning player's name is engraved on it.

question: Which country hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup?
context: First World Cup held in the Middle East and first played in winter (November–December) due to summer heat. Argentina won; Messi's first World Cup title.

question: Which Dutch speed skater won 13 Olympic medals and is the most decorated Dutch Olympian ever?
answer: Ireen Wüst (6 gold, 5 silver, 2 bronze — also the only athlete to win individual gold at five different Olympics)
context: Five consecutive Olympics (2006–2022). Six individual golds across 1500m and team pursuit. Symbol of Dutch long-track speed-skating dominance.

question: What is the most spoken native language in the world?
context: Around 940 million native speakers. English has more total speakers (~1.5 billion including L2), but Mandarin is ahead in native (L1) speakers.

question: What does "RSVP" stand for?
context: Literally 'Respond if you please'. Still used in English and other languages in formal invitations.

question: What is a group of lions called?
context: Typically 10–40 individuals. Lions are the only truly social cat species.

question: What is a group of crows called?
context: From medieval 'terms of venery' — elaborate collective nouns coined partly as a courtly parlour game. Most aren't actually used by zoologists.

question: What is the study of language called?
context: From Latin 'lingua' (tongue). Major branches include phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics and pragmatics.

question: What is the currency of Japan?
context: Symbol ¥, code JPY. Introduced 1871, replacing the complex Edo-period currency system.

question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
context: From Greek 'hex' (six) + 'gonia' (angle). Naturally forms in honeycombs because it's the most efficient shape for tiling a plane.

question: What is the largest mammal on Earth?
context: Up to around 30m long and over 170 tonnes. The largest animal ever known to exist. Its heart alone weighs about 180 kg.

question: What is the dot above a lowercase "i" or "j" called?
context: From Latin 'titulus'. Survives in the English phrase 'to a T' (actually 'to a tittle'). Same root as the dot-distinction between i and ı in Turkish.

question: What does "NASA" stand for?
context: Founded 1958 in response to Sputnik. Civilian agency, distinct from military space programmes.

question: How many official continents are there?
answer: 7 in the traditional model (varies 4–7 depending on system)
context: The seven-continent model is standard in English-speaking countries. Other traditions count six (combining Europe+Asia, or merging the Americas) or five (the Olympic-rings model).

question: What was the currency of the Netherlands before the euro?
context: Replaced by the euro on 1 January 2002. Still legally exchangeable at the Dutch Central Bank until January 2032.

question: In what language was the Qur'an originally written?
context: Classical Arabic of the 7th century. Muslims consider only the original Arabic to be the true Qur'an — translations are treated as interpretations.

question: What is a baby kangaroo called?
context: Applies to all marsupials, not just kangaroos. Joeys are born extremely underdeveloped and continue growing in the pouch for months.

question: What does "PDF" stand for?
context: Created by Adobe in 1993. Became an open standard (ISO 32000) in 2008.

question: What is the official language of Austria?
context: About 8 million speakers. Austrian German has some distinct vocabulary (e.g. Jänner vs Januar) but is mutually intelligible with other varieties.

question: What is the official language of Brazil?
context: Largest Portuguese-speaking country by far (~215 million). Portugal itself has only about 10 million. Brazilian and European Portuguese differ in pronunciation and some vocabulary.

question: Which country has the most native English speakers?
context: Around 240–300 million native speakers. India has more total English speakers (including L2) but fewer natives.

question: What does "ASAP" stand for?
context: First documented use around 1955 in the US military. Often criticised in business writing for being vague about actual urgency.

question: What is the name for the fear of spiders?
context: From Greek 'arachne' (spider, from the myth of Arachne). One of the most common specific phobias, affecting an estimated 3–15% of people.

question: What is the currency of Switzerland?
context: CHF — Confoederatio Helvetica Franc. Switzerland is not in the Eurozone. Generally considered a safe-haven currency.

question: What does "vice versa" mean?
context: Latin, literally 'the position (being) turned'. In use in English since the 17th century.

question: Which letter is used most frequently in English?
context: About 12.7% of all letters in English text. A key fact in cryptography — frequency analysis can break simple substitution ciphers.

question: What does "www" stand for?
context: Created by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN, 1989–1991. Made public and royalty-free in 1993.

question: What is the term for a word that is spelled the same forwards and backwards?
context: From Greek 'palin' (again) + 'dromos' (running). The longest single-word English palindrome is often cited as 'tattarrattat' (coined by Joyce in Ulysses).

question: In which year did World War II begin in Europe?
context: Invasion of Poland began on 1 September 1939. Britain and France declared war on 3 September. The Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact (August 1939) had secretly divided Eastern Europe between Germany and the USSR.

question: In which year did World War II end?
answer: 1945 (V-E Day in May, V-J Day in August/September)
context: V-E Day was 8 May 1945. Japan announced surrender 15 August and formally signed on 2 September aboard USS Missouri — after the atomic bombings of Hiroshima (6 Aug) and Nagasaki (9 Aug).

question: In which year did humans first land on the Moon?
context: Apollo 11 launched 16 July; landed in the Sea of Tranquility on 20 July 1969. Michael Collins stayed in lunar orbit. Twelve people have walked on the Moon in total — all between 1969 and 1972.

question: The American Civil War was fought between which years?
context: Roughly 620,000–750,000 deaths — more than all other US wars combined up to Vietnam. Started at Fort Sumter in April 1861; Lee surrendered at Appomattox in April 1865.

question: The Western Roman Empire is traditionally said to have fallen in which year?
context: Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last Western emperor, Romulus Augustulus. The Eastern (Byzantine) Empire continued for nearly a thousand years more. The 476 date is a convenient marker; the decline was gradual.

question: In which year did Martin Luther post his 95 Theses?
context: Traditionally said to have been nailed to the door of Wittenberg's Castle Church on 31 October 1517. The modern consensus is he probably mailed them to his archbishop that day — the door story may be legend. Triggered the Protestant Reformation.

question: In which year did Columbus first reach the Americas?
context: Landed on Guanahani in the Bahamas on 12 October. Sailed under the Spanish Crown (Ferdinand and Isabella). Never realised he hadn't reached Asia.

question: In which year was Nelson Mandela released from prison?
context: Released on 11 February 1990 by President F.W. de Klerk after 27 years of imprisonment — mostly at Robben Island, then Pollsmoor, then Victor Verster. Won the Nobel Peace Prize with de Klerk in 1993.

question: In which year did apartheid formally end in South Africa?
answer: 1994 (first multiracial democratic elections; Mandela elected president)
context: First democratic election held on 27 April 1994 — now celebrated as Freedom Day. Mandela served one term as president (1994–99). Apartheid laws had been progressively repealed from 1990.

question: Who invented the movable-type printing press in Europe, and roughly when?
context: Around 1440 in Mainz. Printing with movable type existed in China centuries earlier (Bi Sheng, 11th c). Gutenberg's innovation combined metal type, oil-based ink and a wooden press. His 42-line Bible (~1455) was the first major book.

question: In which year did the Soviet Union launch Sputnik 1?
context: Launched 4 October 1957. The first artificial satellite. Triggered the Space Race and led directly to the creation of NASA (1958) and the US push for educational reform.

question: The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in which year?
context: Reactor 4 exploded during a safety test on 26 April 1986. Death toll estimates range wildly — 31 direct deaths, thousands over decades from cancer. The exclusion zone remains largely uninhabitable.

question: In which year was John F. Kennedy assassinated?
context: Shot during his motorcade in Dallas at 12:30 pm. Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested and himself killed by Jack Ruby two days later. The Warren Commission concluded Oswald acted alone; conspiracy theories persist.

question: Who was the first woman in space?
context: Flew on Vostok 6 on 16 June 1963, aged 26. Orbited Earth 48 times. No other woman flew in space until Svetlana Savitskaya in 1982, and no Western woman until Sally Ride in 1983.

question: In which year did the Vietnam War end with the fall of Saigon?
context: North Vietnamese tanks entered Saigon on 30 April 1975. The US evacuated its embassy by helicopter in iconic scenes. The war killed some 3 million Vietnamese and 58,000 US service members.

question: The Korean War was fought between which years?
context: Began when North Korea invaded the South on 25 June 1950. An armistice was signed on 27 July 1953 at Panmunjom — technically the two Koreas are still at war.

question: On what date did Japan attack Pearl Harbor?
context: About 2,400 Americans killed. FDR's 'date which will live in infamy' speech led to US entry into WWII. The US declared war on Japan the next day; Germany declared war on the US on 11 December.

question: In which year was the Soviet Union formally dissolved?
context: Formally dissolved on 26 December 1991, a day after Gorbachev's resignation. Fifteen republics became independent states. End of the Cold War.

question: In which year was William of Orange (Willem van Oranje) assassinated?
context: Shot at the Prinsenhof in Delft by Balthasar Gerards — widely considered the first head of state to be assassinated with a handgun. Started the Orange-Nassau dynasty that still rules the Netherlands.

question: In which year did the Dutch Revolt against Spain begin, marking the start of the Eighty Years' War?
context: Battle of Heiligerlee, 23 May 1568. Led by William the Silent against Philip II of Spain's heavy taxation and religious persecution. Lasted 80 years, ending at the Peace of Westphalia in 1648.

question: In which year was the Battle of Hastings fought?
context: 14 October 1066. William the Conqueror (Duke of Normandy) defeated King Harold II, who was killed — traditionally by an arrow to the eye. Transformed English language, law and society; added thousands of Norman French words to English.

question: How many wives did Henry VIII of England have?
answer: 6 (Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard, Catherine Parr)
context: Mnemonic: 'divorced, beheaded, died, divorced, beheaded, survived'. Only Jane Seymour gave him a male heir (the future Edward VI). His break with Rome — to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon — created the Church of England.

question: In which year was Charlemagne crowned Holy Roman Emperor?
answer: 800 CE (by Pope Leo III in Rome)
context: Crowned by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day in St Peter's Basilica. Reunited much of Western Europe for the first time since Rome. Often called the 'father of Europe'.

question: The Suez Crisis occurred in which year?
context: Egypt's Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal in July 1956. Britain, France and Israel invaded but were forced to withdraw under US and Soviet pressure — usually cited as the moment British and French imperial power ended.

question: What is the Dutch name for the famine in the occupied western Netherlands during the winter of 1944–45?
context: Winter 1944–45. Germans blockaded food into the still-occupied western Netherlands as reprisal for a Dutch railway strike. Around 20,000 died; people ate tulip bulbs. Long-term health effects on in-utero children became a landmark case study in epigenetics.

question: What is the highest mountain above sea level?
context: 8,849m, surveyed by a joint China/Nepal measurement in 2020. Called Sagarmāthā in Nepal and Chomolungma in Tibet. First summit by Hillary and Tenzing Norgay on 29 May 1953.

question: What is the longest river in the world?
answer: The Nile (~6,650 km) — though some studies argue the Amazon is longer; the traditional answer is the Nile.
context: Traditionally about 6,650 km. 2007–08 expeditions claimed the Amazon is around 100 km longer, but the methodology is disputed. Flows through 11 countries to the Mediterranean.

question: Which country has the largest land area?
context: About 17.1 million km² — nearly double second-place Canada. Spans 11 time zones. Around 77% is Asian, though most of the population lives in European Russia.

question: Which country overtook China in 2023 to become the world's most populous?
context: Overtook China in April 2023 per UN estimates. China's population began shrinking in 2022 for the first time since the Mao-era famine.

question: What is the largest lake in the world by surface area?
answer: The Caspian Sea (despite the name, it's technically a lake)
context: About 371,000 km². Endorheic (no outflow). Its legal status — sea or lake — was settled by the five bordering states in 2018 as 'a body of water with special status'.

question: What is the tallest completed building in the world as of 2026?
answer: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai (828m) — held the title since 2010; Jeddah Tower is expected to surpass it around 2028.
context: 828m, held the title since 2010. Designed by Adrian Smith (SOM). The Jeddah Tower in Saudi Arabia, long paused, resumed construction in 2023 and is expected to exceed 1,000m.

question: What is the capital of Brazil?
answer: Brasília (not Rio de Janeiro or São Paulo)
context: The capital moved from Rio to Brasília in 1960 as part of a push to develop the interior. UNESCO World Heritage Site for its modernist planning by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer.

question: What is the capital of Thailand?
context: Its ceremonial full Thai name is the longest place name in the world (around 170 Roman characters): Krung Thep Maha Nakhon…

question: What is the largest island in the world?
answer: Greenland (Australia is counted as a continent, not an island)
context: About 2.17 million km². If Australia were counted as an island, it would be roughly 3.5 times larger. Politically a constituent country of the Kingdom of Denmark.

question: What is the lowest point of land on Earth?
answer: The shore of the Dead Sea (~430m below sea level)
context: Around 430m below sea level and still dropping (roughly 1m per year). Lowest dry-land point on Earth.
option_a: The shore of the Dead Sea

question: Which two countries each share land borders with 14 neighboring countries?
context: Both China and Russia border 14 countries — tied for most in the world.

question: South Africa has three capital cities — what are they?
context: Compromise arrangement from the 1910 Union of South Africa, merging former British colonies and Boer republics. Pretoria hosts the presidency; Cape Town hosts Parliament; Bloemfontein hosts the Supreme Court of Appeal.

question: What is the smallest continent by land area?
answer: Australia (or Oceania, depending on the model used)
context: About 8.6 million km². 'Oceania' (including all Pacific islands) is sometimes used instead. Either way, less than half the size of second-smallest Europe.

question: What is the largest desert on Earth?
answer: Antarctica (classified as a cold desert due to minimal precipitation); the Sahara is the largest *hot* desert
context: About 14 million km². Annual precipitation at the South Pole is under 5cm water equivalent — drier than the Sahara. 'Desert' is a climate definition, not a temperature one.

question: What is the capital of Morocco?
context: Capital since 1912, under the French protectorate. Casablanca is larger and better known economically but Rabat is the seat of government.

question: What is the capital of Vietnam?
context: Capital of unified Vietnam since reunification in 1976. Under French colonial rule it was the capital of French Indochina.

question: What is the current name of the capital of Kazakhstan?
answer: Astana (renamed from Nur-Sultan back to Astana in 2022)
context: Named Astana in 1998, renamed Nur-Sultan in 2019 to honour outgoing president Nursultan Nazarbayev, then reverted to Astana in 2022 after protests against his regime.

question: What is the largest Dutch province by land area?
context: About 5,137 km². Includes the Veluwe national park. Friesland (Fryslân) is close in size. Limburg — your home province — is smaller, ranking 8th among the 12 provinces.

question: How many official languages does Switzerland have?
context: German spoken by about 62%, French by 23%, Italian by 8%, Romansh by under 0.5%. Romansh descends from the Latin of Roman soldiers stationed in the Alps.

question: What strait separates Russia from Alaska?
context: About 82 km wide at its narrowest. Was a land bridge during the last Ice Age — early humans used it to migrate to the Americas. The Diomede Islands (Russia's Big Diomede and the US's Little Diomede) sit under 4 km apart across the International Date Line.

question: Which country is traditionally known as the "Land of the Rising Sun"?
context: 'Nihon' or 'Nippon' literally means 'origin of the sun'. The name reflects Japan's position east of China — from whose perspective the sun rises there.

question: What is the longest international land border in the world?
context: About 8,891 km including Alaska. Often cited as the world's longest undefended border, though that's not strictly accurate — Canada maintains its own border enforcement.

question: Which country has the most lakes?
context: Over two million by some counts, or 879,800 lakes larger than 10 hectares. Contains roughly 20% of the world's fresh water.

question: How many member states does the European Union have as of 2026?
answer: 27 (after the UK's departure in 2020; no new members since Croatia joined in 2013)
context: Lost the UK on Brexit (31 January 2020). No new members since Croatia joined in 2013. Active candidates include Ukraine, Moldova, Türkiye and several Western Balkans states.

question: What is the largest hot desert in the world?
context: About 9 million km² — roughly the size of the United States. Stretches across 11 countries in North Africa.

question: Which planet in our solar system is the hottest?
answer: Venus (surface temperature ~465°C due to runaway greenhouse effect — not Mercury, despite proximity to the Sun)
context: Hotter than Mercury despite being further from the Sun, because of its thick CO₂ atmosphere and runaway greenhouse effect. Surface pressure is around 92 times Earth's.

question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
context: Orbits the Sun in just 88 Earth days. No atmosphere and extreme temperature swings (around –170°C to 430°C).

question: Which planet is nicknamed "the Red Planet"?
context: Iron oxide dust gives the surface its colour. Mars has the largest volcano (Olympus Mons, about 22 km tall) and canyon (Valles Marineris, roughly 4,000 km long) in the solar system.

question: How old is the universe, approximately?
context: From the Planck mission data and refinements: 13.787 ± 0.020 billion years. Earth and the Sun are around 4.5 billion years old — so the universe is about three times older than our solar system.

question: How many planets are in our solar system (as of the current IAU definition)?
context: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune. Pluto was demoted in 2006 under the new IAU rules.

question: In which year was Pluto reclassified as a dwarf planet?
context: Reclassified at the IAU General Assembly in Prague on 24 August 2006. Under the new rules, a planet must clear its orbital neighbourhood — Pluto hasn't. It's now one of five recognised dwarf planets.

question: What is the boiling point of water at sea level?
context: By definition at 1 atmosphere. Boils at a lower temperature at altitude (about 93°C at 2,000m). Fun fact: the original Celsius scale was inverted — 0 was boiling and 100 freezing.

question: What is absolute zero?
context: –273.15°C = 0K. At this temperature molecular motion would theoretically cease. Cannot actually be reached, only approached (current lab record: billionths of a kelvin).

question: What is the chemical symbol for iron?
context: From Latin 'ferrum'. Element 26. Earth's core is largely iron and nickel. Iron is the most abundant element by mass in the Earth as a whole.

question: What is the chemical symbol for silver?
context: From Latin 'argentum'. Element 47. The best electrical and thermal conductor of all elements.

question: What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
context: From Latin 'natrium' (itself from Arabic 'natrun'). Element 11. Reacts violently with water. Table salt is sodium chloride (NaCl).

question: What is the largest internal organ in the human body?
answer: The liver (skin is the largest organ overall)
context: Around 1.5 kg. The only human organ that can regenerate from as little as 25% of its tissue. Performs over 500 distinct functions.

question: How many teeth does a full adult human set typically include?
context: Including four wisdom teeth (third molars). Many modern humans either don't develop wisdom teeth or have them extracted. Children have 20 deciduous ('baby') teeth.

question: How many blood types are in the ABO system?
context: A, B, AB, O. Further split by Rh factor (positive/negative) giving eight common combinations. Discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1901 (Nobel 1930).

question: Roughly what is the speed of sound in air at sea level?
context: About 343 m/s in air at 20°C. Faster in water (~1,480 m/s) and faster still in steel (~5,100 m/s). This is why you see lightning before you hear thunder.

question: What was Charles Darwin's seminal 1859 work?
context: Full title: 'On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection'. Published 24 November 1859. The first print run of 1,250 sold out on day one.

question: What primarily causes ocean tides?
answer: The gravitational pull of the Moon (and to a lesser extent the Sun)
context: The Moon's pull creates two tidal bulges — one facing it, one on the opposite side. Solar tides are about 46% as strong. Spring tides form when Sun and Moon align; neap tides when they're at right angles.
option_a: The gravitational pull of the Sun alone
option_d: The gravitational pull of the Moon (primarily) and the Sun

question: Who is considered the father of genetics?
answer: Gregor Mendel (Austrian monk; his pea-plant experiments were published in 1866)
context: Moravian friar who bred around 28,000 pea plants at the Abbey of St Thomas in Brno. His 1866 paper was largely ignored until rediscovered around 1900.

question: What is the largest land animal on Earth?
context: Up to around 6 tonnes and 4m tall at the shoulder. Gestation lasts about 22 months — the longest of any mammal.

question: What is the tallest land animal?
context: Up to around 5.8m tall. Remarkably, its neck has the same seven cervical vertebrae as almost all mammals — they're just massively elongated. Tongue is about 45cm long and bluish-black.

question: Who proposed the heliocentric model of the solar system in 1543?
context: Published 'De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium' in 1543, in the last year of his life. The Church initially ignored it, then banned it in 1616 once Galileo's advocacy made it controversial.

question: What is pi (π) to five decimal places?
context: 3.14159. π is irrational (never ends, never repeats) and transcendental. 40 digits of π are enough to calculate the circumference of the visible universe to the width of a hydrogen atom.

question: What is the acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface?
context: About 9.81 m/s² (varies slightly by location — lower at the equator, higher at the poles). Galileo established that in vacuum, objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass.

question: A light-year is a unit of what?
answer: Distance (the distance light travels in one year, ~9.46 trillion km)
context: About 9.46 trillion km. The nearest star system, Alpha Centauri, is around 4.24 light-years away. Widely confused with a unit of time by the public.

question: What is the smallest bone in the human body?
context: About 3mm long. One of three middle-ear ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) that transmit sound to the inner ear. Smallest of the 206 bones.

question: Who wrote "Moby-Dick"?
context: Published 1851. Sold poorly in Melville's lifetime; rediscovered in the 1920s and now canonical. Opening line 'Call me Ishmael' is among the most quoted in English literature.

question: Who wrote "The Great Gatsby"?
context: Published 1925. Sold modestly in Fitzgerald's lifetime — his reputation grew after the 1942 Armed Services Edition distributed to US troops. Often read as a critique of the American Dream.

question: In which country is Shakespeare's "Hamlet" set?
context: Set at Elsinore (Kronborg Castle at Helsingør), which exists — though Shakespeare probably never visited Denmark. The story draws on the legend of Amleth from the Danish chronicler Saxo Grammaticus.

question: Who wrote "Ulysses"?
context: Published 1922. The action takes place over a single day — 16 June 1904 — in Dublin, now celebrated as Bloomsday. Banned in the US until 1933 and in the UK until 1936.

question: Who wrote "Madame Bovary"?
context: Published 1856. Flaubert was tried for obscenity in 1857 and acquitted — which turned the book into a bestseller. Pioneered the free indirect discourse that became standard in modern fiction.

question: Who wrote "The Stranger" (L'Étranger)?
context: Published 1942 in occupied France. Opens with 'Aujourd'hui, maman est morte.' A foundational text of absurdism. Camus won the Nobel in 1957.

question: Who wrote "Lolita"?
context: Published 1955 in Paris after US and UK publishers rejected it. The voice of Humbert Humbert is widely considered a landmark of English prose — a uniquely troubling achievement given the subject matter. Nabokov was Russian-born.

question: Which 1860 Dutch novel by Multatuli exposed abuses in the Dutch East Indies?
answer: Max Havelaar (Multatuli was the pen name of Eduard Douwes Dekker)
context: Published 1860. Exposed the exploitation of Javanese peasants under the Dutch 'cultivation system'. Often credited with shifting Dutch public opinion and colonial policy. 'Multatuli' is Latin for 'I have suffered much'.

question: Who wrote "To Kill a Mockingbird"?
context: Won the 1961 Pulitzer Prize. Lee published only one other novel — 'Go Set a Watchman' (2015), a controversial early draft of Mockingbird released in contested circumstances.

question: Who wrote "The Picture of Dorian Gray"?
context: Published 1890. Later used as evidence against Wilde in his 1895 gross-indecency trial — homoerotic undertones were weaponised. Wilde's only novel.

question: Who wrote "The Old Man and the Sea"?
context: Published 1952. Won the Pulitzer in 1953 and the Nobel in 1954. Hemingway's last major work published in his lifetime; he died by suicide in 1961.

question: Who wrote "The Little Prince"?
context: Published 1943 in both French and English, in New York while Saint-Exupéry was exiled during WWII. Over 150 million copies sold. The author disappeared on a reconnaissance flight in 1944.

question: Who created Sherlock Holmes?
context: Created in 1887 with 'A Study in Scarlet'. Doyle killed Holmes off in 1893 at the Reichenbach Falls, then brought him back by popular demand in 1901. Holmes has been adapted more than any other fictional character.

question: Who wrote "Animal Farm"?
context: Published 1945. Satirical allegory of the Russian Revolution. Initially rejected by several publishers worried about offending wartime ally Stalin. 'All animals are equal, but some are more equal than others.'

question: Who wrote "Waiting for Godot"?
context: Premiered in Paris in 1953, in French. Two tramps wait for someone who never arrives. Beckett won the Nobel in 1969 and refused to attend the ceremony.

question: Who wrote "The Handmaid's Tale"?
context: Published 1985. Atwood has said every atrocity in the book had happened historically somewhere. The Emmy-winning 2017 Hulu adaptation turned it into a cultural reference point again.

question: "The Tale of Genji," often called the first novel in world literature, was written in 11th-century Japan by whom?
context: Heian-era court lady; exact dates of birth and death uncertain. 'The Tale of Genji' (~1008) is a 54-chapter psychological novel widely considered the world's first novel.

question: Which Dutch author wrote "Oeroeg" (1948)?
context: Published anonymously in 1948 as a 'Boekenweekgeschenk' — novella about a Dutch boy's friendship with a Javanese boy in colonial Indonesia. Haasse was one of the 20th century's major Dutch authors.

question: Who wrote "The Metamorphosis"?
context: Published 1915. Gregor Samsa wakes transformed into a giant insect (the species left deliberately ambiguous in the original German 'Ungeziefer'). Kafka asked his literary executor Max Brod to burn his unpublished work — Brod refused.

question: Who wrote "The Cherry Orchard"?
context: Premiered January 1904, six months before Chekhov's death at 44. A tragicomedy about a Russian aristocratic family losing their estate. Chekhov considered it a comedy; Stanislavski staged it as a tragedy — a disagreement that shaped modern theatre.

question: Which Edgar Allan Poe poem features the repeated line "Nevermore"?
context: Published January 1845. Made Poe famous but earned him only about $9. 'Quoth the Raven, Nevermore.'

question: Who wrote "The Count of Monte Cristo"?
context: Published 1844–46. A sprawling revenge story — Edmond Dantès, unjustly imprisoned in the Château d'If, escapes to exact methodical revenge. Dumas was of mixed heritage — his grandmother was Haitian.

question: Who received the first Nobel Prize in Literature, awarded in 1901?
context: Today largely forgotten as a poet. The choice was controversial even then — many felt Tolstoy should have received it. Tolstoy never won.

question: Of what nationality was the writer Jorge Luis Borges?
context: Argentine, 1899–1986. Published mostly short stories and essays rather than novels — 'Ficciones' (1944), 'The Aleph' (1949). Never won the Nobel despite being a perennial favourite.

question: Which Dutch author wrote "Rituelen" (Rituals, 1980)?
context: Published 1980. Won the Mobil Pegasus Prize and brought Nooteboom to international audiences. He's now among the most translated contemporary Dutch authors.

question: Which film won Best Picture at the 2026 Oscars?
answer: "One Battle After Another" (directed by Paul Thomas Anderson)
context: Loosely adapted from Thomas Pynchon's 1990 novel 'Vineland'. Leonardo DiCaprio in the lead role. Won Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Adapted Screenplay at the 2026 Oscars.

question: Who was the first actor to play James Bond in the official Eon film series?
context: Scottish. Played Bond in seven films (six official Eon productions, plus the rogue 'Never Say Never Again'). Successors: Lazenby, Moore, Dalton, Brosnan, Craig, with Aaron Taylor-Johnson long rumoured next.

question: Which three films are tied for the most Oscar wins, with 11 each?
answer: Ben-Hur (1959), Titanic (1997), and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King (2003)
context: Ben-Hur (1959), Titanic (1997), and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King (2003). Return of the King is the only one to win every category it was nominated for (11/11).
option_b: Ben-Hur, West Side Story, and The Return of the King
option_c: Ben-Hur, Titanic, and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
option_d: Gone with the Wind, Titanic, and Schindler's List

question: Who directed "Citizen Kane"?
context: Released 1941. Welles was 25. Loosely based on William Randolph Hearst, who retaliated by banning mentions in his newspapers. Topped 'greatest films' critics' lists for decades.

question: Who directed "Jaws"?
context: Considered the first modern blockbuster. Famously over-budget and delayed because the mechanical shark kept breaking — Spielberg showed less of it as a result, which increased the suspense.

question: Who directed "Inception"?
context: Original script; Nolan developed the idea for about 10 years. Hans Zimmer's 'BRAAAM' sound set a template for a decade of film trailers.

question: Who composed "Messiah"?
context: Composed in about 3–4 weeks in 1741. Premiered in Dublin 1742. The tradition of audiences standing for the Hallelujah Chorus allegedly began when George II rose during a London performance.

question: Who composed the Brandenburg Concertos?
context: A set of six concertos (1721) dedicated to the Margrave of Brandenburg, who seemingly never even had them performed. They sat in a Berlin library until the 19th century.

question: Who composed "La Traviata"?
context: Premiered 1853 in Venice. Based on Dumas fils's 'La Dame aux Camélias'. One of the most-performed operas in the world.

question: Which musician won the 2016 Nobel Prize in Literature?
answer: Bob Dylan (for "poetic expressions within the great American song tradition")
context: First musician to win the Literature Nobel. The Academy reportedly had trouble contacting him to inform him. He initially didn't respond publicly, which prompted much commentary.

question: Michael Jackson's album "Thriller" was released in which year?
answer: 1982 (often cited as the best-selling album of all time)
context: Released 30 November 1982. Best-selling album of all time — around 70 million copies. Jackson's collaboration with Quincy Jones. The 1983 'Thriller' music video redefined the form.

question: Which alter ego did David Bowie create in 1972?
context: Created for the album 'The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars' (1972). Bowie retired Ziggy on stage at the Hammersmith Odeon in July 1973.

question: What is the nationality of ABBA?
context: Agnetha, Björn, Benny, Anni-Frid. Won Eurovision 1974 with 'Waterloo'. Reunited in 2021 for the album 'Voyage' — their first in 40 years.

question: The musical "Mamma Mia!" is built around songs by which band?
context: Premiered London 1999. Over 60 million tickets sold worldwide. The 2008 film starring Meryl Streep grossed about $614M.

question: Which two countries are tied with the most Eurovision Song Contest wins (7 each) as of 2024?
Who was the voice of Darth Vader in the original Star Wars trilogy?
context: Voiced Vader but was uncredited in the original film. The on-screen body was played by David Prowse. Jones also voiced Mufasa in The Lion King.

question: What is the longest-running American animated primetime TV series?
context: First aired 17 December 1989. 36+ seasons, 760+ episodes. Famous for seeming to have predicted a number of events, including a Trump presidency in a 2000 episode.

question: Which TV series had the most-watched finale in US history?
context: 28 February 1983. About 106 million US viewers — roughly 60% of the country. Held the single most-watched broadcast record for 25+ years.

question: What was Netflix's first in-house original scripted series?
context: February 2013. US remake of a 1990 BBC series. Released all 13 episodes at once, establishing the Netflix binge-release model. Lead actor Kevin Spacey was later written out after sexual-misconduct allegations.

question: Which Dutch director made "RoboCop" (1987) and "Basic Instinct" (1992) in Hollywood?
context: Dutch. Also made 'Soldaat van Oranje' (1977), 'Black Book' (2006) and 'Elle' (2016). Known for combining violence, sexuality and political satire.

question: Who holds the record for most Best Actor Oscar wins, with three?
context: Three Best Actor Oscars: 'My Left Foot' (1989), 'There Will Be Blood' (2007), 'Lincoln' (2012). Famous for extreme method preparation. Retired in 2017 before returning to the screen.

question: Which Italian composer wrote "The Barber of Seville"?
context: Premiered 1816 in Rome. Rossini composed it in under three weeks. Based on the first of Beaumarchais' Figaro plays — Mozart's 'Marriage of Figaro' is based on the second.

question: What is the highest-grossing film of all time (unadjusted for inflation) as of 2026?
answer: Avatar (2009), which retook the top spot from Avengers: Endgame after a 2022 re-release
context: Cameron's 2009 film; the 2022 re-release pushed it back ahead of Avengers: Endgame. Drove a 3D cinema boom that has since largely receded.

question: Which instrument is Frédéric Chopin most associated with?
context: Wrote almost exclusively for solo piano. Polish-French, 1810–49. Died at 39, most likely of tuberculosis.

question: Who directed the 2026 Best Picture winner "One Battle After Another"?
answer: Paul Thomas Anderson (his first Oscar wins — also took Best Director and Best Adapted Screenplay)
context: Anderson's 11th film. Swept the top categories. He had been nominated eight times previously without winning.

question: Who painted "The Last Supper"?
answer: Leonardo da Vinci (1495–98, in Milan's Santa Maria delle Grazie)
context: Tempera and oil on dry plaster — Leonardo's experimental technique meant it began deteriorating almost immediately. Restored repeatedly, most recently from 1978–1999.

question: Who painted "The Starry Night"?
answer: Vincent van Gogh (1889, from the asylum at Saint-Rémy)
context: Painted June 1889, a year before Van Gogh's death. From memory of the view from his asylum window at Saint-Paul-de-Mausole. Held at MoMA in New York.

question: Of what nationality was the painter Frida Kahlo?
context: 1907–1954. Contracted polio as a child; survived a horrific bus accident at 18 that left lifelong injuries. Of her ~143 paintings, the majority were self-portraits.

question: Who sculpted "The Thinker"?
context: Originally part of Rodin's 'The Gates of Hell' (commissioned 1880) depicting Dante. Later separated into a standalone work. Roughly 28 full-sized bronze casts exist worldwide.

question: To which artistic tradition does Peter Paul Rubens belong?
context: 1577–1640. Prolific — around 1,400 works, produced in a large workshop. Known for voluptuous nudes (hence 'Rubensian' as an adjective).

question: Which pop artist is famous for his Campbell's Soup Cans?
context: 1962 series of 32 canvases, one for each Campbell's variety. Pioneered the idea that advertising imagery could be fine art. Warhol's 'Factory' studio was a cultural hub in 1960s New York.

question: Which American painter is famous for his drip/action painting technique?
context: 1912–1956. Developed 'drip' painting around 1947 at his Long Island studio. Died in a car crash at 44. Central figure in Abstract Expressionism.

question: What is the pseudonymous name of the anonymous British street artist known for politically charged stencil work?
context: Identity officially unconfirmed; most likely Robin Gunningham, born Bristol around 1973. 'Girl with Balloon' famously self-destructed halfway through shredding at Sotheby's in 2018 — and then sold for even more.

question: Who designed the Guggenheim Museum in Bilbao (opened 1997)?
context: Opened 1997. Deconstructivist titanium-clad design revitalised the city — the 'Bilbao effect' is now a term in urban planning. Gehry is Canadian-American.

question: The Eiffel Tower was built as the entrance arch for which 1889 event?
