answer: The Exposition Universelle (Paris World's Fair), marking the centenary of the French Revolution
context: Built by Gustave Eiffel's company for the centenary of the Revolution. Originally intended to be dismantled after 20 years; kept because it proved useful as a radio antenna.

question: Who painted the Ghent Altarpiece (also known as "The Adoration of the Mystic Lamb")?
answer: Jan van Eyck (completed 1432), with work begun by his brother Hubert
context: Completed 1432. 12-panel polyptych in Ghent's St Bavo Cathedral. Often cited as the most-stolen artwork of all time (13 separate thefts). The 'Just Judges' panel stolen in 1934 has never been recovered.

question: Which Belgian surrealist is famous for paintings of men in bowler hats, pipes, and apples?
context: 1898–1967. Most famous works: 'The Son of Man' (bowler hat with floating apple), 'The Treachery of Images' ('Ceci n'est pas une pipe'), and 'The Lovers' (the cloth-shrouded kiss).

question: In which city is the Hermitage Museum?
context: Founded in 1764 by Catherine the Great. One of the largest museums in the world — around 3 million items. The main building is the Winter Palace.

question: In which city is the Uffizi Gallery?
context: Originally a Medici office building — 'uffizi' means 'offices'. Houses Botticelli's 'Birth of Venus' and 'Primavera', da Vinci's 'Annunciation' and much more.

question: In which city is Notre-Dame Cathedral located?
context: Built 1163–1345. Badly damaged by fire on 15 April 2019. Reopened in December 2024 after a restoration costing over €700M.

question: In which city is the Colosseum?
context: Completed 80 CE. Could hold an estimated 50,000–80,000 spectators. Hosted gladiatorial combat, staged hunts and occasional mock naval battles with a flooded arena.

question: In which city is the Parthenon?
context: Built 447–432 BCE under Pericles. Dedicated to Athena Parthenos. Badly damaged in 1687 when Venetian bombardment hit an Ottoman powder magazine stored inside it.

question: Which Japanese artist made "The Great Wave off Kanagawa"?
context: Woodblock print, part of 'Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji'. Hokusai was around 70 when he made it. Heavily influenced European Impressionism via the Japonisme movement.

question: Who painted "The Kiss" (1907–08), with its gold-leaf embrace?
context: 1907–08. Part of Klimt's 'Golden Period' — actual gold leaf used. Held at the Österreichische Galerie Belvedere in Vienna.

question: Frida Kahlo's husband was a famous Mexican muralist — who was he?
context: 1886–1957. Married Kahlo in 1929; they divorced in 1939 and remarried in 1940. His massive public murals celebrated Mexican workers and pre-Columbian heritage. A committed Marxist — he once sheltered Trotsky.

question: What was the nationality of the architect Le Corbusier?
context: Born Charles-Édouard Jeanneret-Gris in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Switzerland, 1887. Later naturalised French. Influential but controversial — pioneered brutalist/modernist urban planning (Ville Radieuse), often criticised for its anti-human scale.

question: In which Madrid museum is Picasso's "Guernica" displayed?
context: Guernica arrived at the Reina Sofía from MoMA in 1981 per Picasso's wishes, which required Franco's death. The museum also houses major works by Dalí and Miró.

question: Which Dutch/Flemish painter is famous for peasant scenes and "The Tower of Babel"?
context: Around 1525–1569. Father of a Flemish painting dynasty (Pieter the Younger, Jan the Elder). 'The Tower of Babel' (1563) and 'Hunters in the Snow' (1565) are among his best-known works.

question: Which Dutch architect designed the Rietveld Schröder House (1924), a De Stijl landmark in Utrecht?
context: 1888–1964. Trained as a cabinetmaker. His Red and Blue Chair (1917) is a De Stijl design classic. The Rietveld Schröder House is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: Who designed the glass pyramid at the Louvre (completed 1989)?
context: Commissioned by President Mitterrand. Met with fierce opposition initially — now beloved. Pei was Chinese-American; also designed the Bank of China Tower in Hong Kong and the National Gallery East Wing in Washington DC.

question: Which country did Lionel Messi captain to victory at the 2022 FIFA World Cup?
context: Beat France 3–3 (4–2 on penalties) in one of the greatest finals ever played. Messi's long-awaited World Cup, at age 35.

question: Which country won UEFA Euro 2024?
answer: Spain (a record 4th European Championship title, beating England 2–1 in Berlin)
context: Final played in Berlin. Spain beat England 2–1 to take a record fourth European title.

question: How many Tour de France overall wins do the all-time co-leaders have?
answer: 5 each — Jacques Anquetil, Eddy Merckx, Bernard Hinault, and Miguel Indurain (Lance Armstrong's 7 were stripped)
context: Anquetil (1957, 1961–64), Merckx (1969–72, 1974), Hinault (1978–79, 1981–82, 1985), Indurain (1991–95 consecutive). Armstrong's seven wins (1999–2005) were stripped in 2012 for doping.

question: Who holds the men's all-time record for international football goals?
answer: Cristiano Ronaldo (Portugal) — over 140 and still rising as of 2026
context: Over 140 international goals. The gap to second-place Ali Daei (109) is substantial — and Ronaldo is still adding to it.

question: Who holds the men's all-time record for Grand Slam singles titles?
context: Passed Nadal (22) and Federer (20) in 2023. Ten Australian Open titles alone. Longevity is the defining feature of his career.

question: What is the oldest of the four tennis Grand Slam tournaments?
context: First tournament in 1877 had 22 competitors. Now the most prestigious event in tennis.

question: Who has won the most Olympic medals of any athlete in history?
answer: Michael Phelps (28 total, 23 gold — US swimmer)
context: 28 total Olympic medals; 23 gold. Four Olympics (2004–16). More individual Olympic golds (13) than any athlete since Leonidas of Rhodes (152 BCE).

question: How many FIFA World Cups did Pelé win?
context: 1958 (aged 17), 1962 and 1970 — the only man to win three World Cups as a player. Real name Edson Arantes do Nascimento.

question: Which NBA franchise has won the most championships?
answer: The Boston Celtics (18, after the 2024 title)
context: Won their 18th title in 2024, breaking a tie with the LA Lakers (17). First NBA franchise to reach 18.

question: Who set the men's marathon world record at Chicago in October 2023?
answer: Kelvin Kiptum of Kenya (2:00:35); he died in a road accident in February 2024
context: 2:00:35 at Chicago, 8 October 2023. Broke Eliud Kipchoge's record by 34 seconds. Killed in a road accident in Kenya on 11 February 2024, aged 24 — a major loss to the sport.

question: Which country won the first FIFA Women's World Cup in 1991?
context: Inaugural tournament in China. The USWNT has since won four titles total (1991, 1999, 2015, 2019) — more than any other country.

question: Which Dutch footballer popularised "Total Football" as both a player and coach?
context: Dutch style in which every outfield player is comfortable in any position. Developed at Ajax under coach Rinus Michels, then transferred to Barcelona by Cruyff as player and coach. Cruyff died in 2016.

question: Who won the 2025 Men's Ballon d'Or?
context: Based on his explosive 2024–25 season with PSG. Ended a Messi/Ronaldo-dominated era. First Frenchman to win since Benzema (2022).

question: How many consecutive Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships did Max Verstappen win from 2021 to 2024?
context: 2021–2024 consecutively. Lando Norris (McLaren) broke the streak in 2025, becoming the first British champion since Hamilton in 2020.

question: What is Usain Bolt's world record for the men's 100m?
context: Set in Berlin at the World Championships on 16 August 2009. Often cited as near the physical limit of modern sprinting. Bolt retired in 2017.

question: Who was the first winner of the Ballon d'Or in 1956?
context: Won at age 41 — still the oldest winner ever. Played for Blackpool and Stoke City. Kept playing top-flight English football into his 50s.

question: Which country won the 2023 Rugby World Cup (their 4th title)?
context: Fourth Rugby World Cup title (1995, 2007, 2019, 2023), making them the most successful rugby nation. Won all three 2023 knockout games by a single point.

question: At which Olympics did skateboarding make its Games debut?
context: Debut at Tokyo 2020 (held in 2021 due to COVID). Youngest gold medallist was Momiji Nishiya of Japan, aged 13.

question: How many players does a volleyball team have on court at once?
context: Plus a libero (defensive specialist) who can substitute freely in back-row positions. Beach volleyball has just two per side.

question: Which city will host the 2028 Summer Olympics?
context: LA's third Olympics (1932, 1984, 2028). Will reportedly use almost entirely existing venues.

question: Which three countries are jointly hosting the 2026 Men's FIFA World Cup?
answer: The United States, Canada, and Mexico
context: First World Cup jointly hosted by three countries. Also the first with 48 teams (up from 32). Final in New Jersey.
option_a: The United States, Canada, and Mexico
option_b: The United States, Mexico, and Brazil
option_c: Canada, the United States, and Argentina
option_d: Mexico, the United States, and Colombia

question: Who holds the men's long jump world record, set at the 1991 World Championships in Tokyo?
context: Still stands after 30+ years — one of the longest-held records in athletics. Broke Bob Beamon's legendary 1968 Mexico City mark of 8.90m.

question: Which Dutch player won the Wimbledon men's singles title in 1996?
context: Beat MaliVai Washington in the final. Still the only Dutchman to have won a Grand Slam men's singles title.

question: How many holes are in a standard round of golf?
context: Standardised by the Royal and Ancient at St Andrews (Scotland) in 1858. Total par is typically 72.

question: Who is the only tennis player to win a "Golden Slam" (all four majors plus Olympic gold in the same calendar year)?
context: 1988 — Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open, plus Olympic gold in Seoul. No one else — man or woman — has done it in a single calendar year.

question: What is the currency of the United Kingdom?
context: £, GBP. The world's oldest currency still in use, dating to roughly 775 CE — predating the Bank of England (founded 1694).

question: What is the currency of South Africa?
context: Named after the Witwatersrand, the gold-bearing ridge at Johannesburg. Introduced in 1961, replacing the pound.

question: What is the currency of Sweden?
context: Introduced in 1873 as part of the Scandinavian Monetary Union with Denmark and Norway. Sweden has still not adopted the euro despite being obliged to under EU treaty.

question: What is the currency of Norway?
context: Also dates to the 1873 Scandinavian Monetary Union. Norway is not in the EU — referendums in 1972 and 1994 both went against membership.

question: What is a group of wolves called?
context: Wolves are highly social; packs are usually family units of around 5–10 individuals. Social hierarchy is less rigid than the outdated 'alpha male' model suggested.

question: What is a group of fish called?
context: 'Shoal' = any loose grouping; 'school' = coordinated synchronised movement. From Dutch 'schole' meaning 'troop' — the same root as 'school' for education.

question: What is a group of owls called?
context: From medieval 'terms of venery'. The phrase was popularised by T.H. White's 'The Sword in the Stone' and later adopted more widely.

question: What is a baby frog called?
context: Also sometimes called a polliwog. Breathes through gills before metamorphosis, then develops lungs and loses the tail.

question: What is a baby pig called?
context: Or 'shoat'. Pigs are among the most intelligent domesticated animals — generally placed above dogs on standard cognition tests.

question: What is a word with the opposite meaning of another called?
context: From Greek 'anti' (against) + 'onoma' (name).

question: What is a word with the same or similar meaning to another called?
context: From Greek 'syn' (together) + 'onoma' (name). True synonyms are rare in any language — most near-synonyms carry slightly different connotations.

question: What is the fear of heights called?
context: From Greek 'akron' (peak or summit). Affects some 5% of people severely. Distinct from vertigo, which is a medical condition.

question: What is the fear of enclosed spaces called?
context: From Latin 'claustrum' (enclosed place). Affects an estimated 5–7% of people. Commonly triggered by MRI machines, lifts or small rooms.

question: How many official languages does Switzerland have at the federal level?
context: German, French, Italian, Romansh at federal level. Romansh received full federal language status in 1938 — partly to counter Italian fascist claims on Grisons.

question: In which year was NATO founded?
context: Signed in Washington on 4 April 1949 by 12 founding members. Now 32 members after Sweden's 2024 accession. Article 5 (collective defence) has only ever been invoked once — after 9/11.

question: In which year was the United Nations founded?
context: Founded 24 October 1945 (UN Day) after the San Francisco Conference. Replaced the failed League of Nations. Currently 193 member states.

question: How many member states does the European Union have in 2026?
context: 27 since the UK left in 2020. No new members have joined since Croatia in 2013.

question: How many sides does a pentagon have?
context: From Greek 'pente' (five) + 'gonia' (angle). The US Department of Defense headquarters (the Pentagon) is named after its shape.

question: How many players does a basketball team have on the court at once?
context: Plus seven substitutes on the bench. Basketball was invented in 1891 by James Naismith at a YMCA in Springfield, Massachusetts.

question: What is the famous 45-letter "longest word" referring to a lung disease caused by inhaling fine silica dust?
context: 45 letters. Refers to silicosis. Often dismissed as coined specifically to be 'the longest word' rather than for genuine medical use.

question: What is the currency of Mexico?
context: MXN. Adopted 1863, based on the Spanish silver peso. The dollar sign ($) actually derives from the Spanish peso's symbol.

question: What is the currency of India?
context: INR. From the Sanskrit 'rūpya', meaning 'wrought silver'. The rupee sign (₹) was officially introduced in 2010.

question: How many countries use the euro as their official currency (eurozone) as of 2026?
context: 20 countries: Austria, Belgium, Croatia, Cyprus, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Portugal, Slovakia, Slovenia, Spain.

question: What does "CEO" stand for?
context: Most senior executive of a company. Originated in US corporate usage in the mid-20th century and became standard worldwide.

question: What does "DIY" stand for?
context: Rose to popularity in the 1950s–60s along with suburban home ownership. Often contrasted with 'DIFM' (Do It For Me).

question: Who was the last pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt?
context: Died by suicide on 10 or 12 August 30 BCE, reportedly from an asp bite, after Mark Antony's defeat at Actium. Egypt became a Roman province. She was Macedonian Greek by descent — the Ptolemaic dynasty had ruled Egypt since Alexander's general Ptolemy I founded it in 305 BCE.

question: In which year did Alexander the Great die?
context: Died in Babylon on 10 or 11 June 323 BCE, aged 32 — most likely of fever, possibly typhoid or malaria. His empire fragmented almost immediately into warring successor states (Diadochi). Had conquered territory from Greece to the Indus Valley.

question: On which date was Julius Caesar assassinated?
context: Stabbed 23 times in the Theatre of Pompey by a group of senators including Brutus and Cassius. His grand-nephew Octavian (later Augustus) avenged him and ended the Republic. Shakespeare's phrase 'beware the Ides of March' comes from Plutarch.

question: In which year was the Battle of Thermopylae fought?
answer: 480 BCE (Greeks under Leonidas vs. Persia under Xerxes)
context: A few hundred Greeks (famously including 300 Spartans) held a narrow pass for three days against a vastly larger Persian force. Ultimately lost, but bought time for the Greek cities. Later balanced by the naval victory at Salamis that same year.

question: In which year did Genghis Khan found the Mongol Empire?
context: Proclaimed Khan at the kurultai of 1206, uniting the Mongol tribes. His empire became the largest contiguous land empire in history — at its peak around 24 million km². Died 1227.

question: During which years did the Black Death peak in Europe?
context: Killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe's population — between 75 and 200 million people worldwide. Caused by Yersinia pestis, transmitted via fleas on rats. Triggered huge labour shortages that reshaped European society.

question: Who discovered Tutankhamun's tomb, and in which year?
answer: Howard Carter, 1922 (in the Valley of the Kings)
context: Carter's team opened the tomb on 4 November 1922 — remarkably intact because it had been hidden under later construction debris. Tutankhamun himself was a relatively minor pharaoh; the tomb's fame comes from its preserved contents.

question: In which year was the US Declaration of Independence signed?
context: Adopted by the Continental Congress on 4 July 1776. Primarily drafted by Thomas Jefferson. The actual war for independence continued until 1783 (Treaty of Paris).

question: On what date did the D-Day Normandy landings take place?
context: Operation Overlord. Over 156,000 Allied troops landed on five Normandy beaches (Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, Sword). Largest seaborne invasion in history. Marked the beginning of the liberation of Western Europe.

question: In which month and year were atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki?
answer: August 1945 (Hiroshima on the 6th, Nagasaki on the 9th)
context: Hiroshima on 6 August, Nagasaki on 9 August 1945. Only use of nuclear weapons in war. Killed an estimated 129,000–226,000 people, mostly civilians. Japan surrendered on 15 August.

question: On what date was the Auschwitz concentration camp liberated?
answer: 27 January 1945 (by Soviet forces — now International Holocaust Remembrance Day)
context: Liberated by the Red Army. Over 1.1 million people had been murdered there — the single deadliest Nazi camp. Now the date of International Holocaust Remembrance Day, established by the UN in 2005.

question: In which year did the 9/11 terrorist attacks occur?
context: Four hijacked planes; two into the World Trade Center, one into the Pentagon, one crashed in Pennsylvania. Killed 2,977 people. Led directly to the US wars in Afghanistan and Iraq and reshaped global security policy.

question: Who was the first Black president of the United States?
context: Born 1961 in Honolulu. Son of a Kenyan father and American mother. Served two terms (2009–2017). Won the Nobel Peace Prize in his first year — a controversial early award.

question: In which month and year did Queen Elizabeth II die?
context: Died at Balmoral Castle, aged 96. Charles III acceded immediately. State funeral on 19 September 2022 was one of the most-watched broadcasts in history.

question: In which year did Japan's Meiji Restoration begin?
context: Restored imperial power under Emperor Meiji, ending the Tokugawa shogunate. Rapidly industrialised Japan using Western models. Within 40 years Japan went from feudal isolation to defeating Russia in 1905.

question: Which Spanish conquistador led the conquest of the Aztec Empire, completed in 1521?
context: Landed 1519 with about 500 men. Exploited a revolt against Aztec overlord Moctezuma II by subject peoples. Disease (especially smallpox, to which Indigenous people had no immunity) did as much work as swords. Tenochtitlan fell August 1521.

question: Which Spanish conquistador led the conquest of the Incan Empire, completed in 1533?
context: Captured Inca emperor Atahualpa at Cajamarca in 1532 and ransomed, then killed him. Cuzco fell 1533. Pizarro was himself assassinated in 1541 by factional rivals in Lima.

question: Which Viking explorer is credited with reaching North America (Vinland) around the year 1000?
context: Reached 'Vinland' (likely Newfoundland) around the year 1000 — roughly 500 years before Columbus. Norse ruins at L'Anse aux Meadows were confirmed archaeologically in 1960.

question: In which year were the first ancient Olympic Games traditionally said to have been held?
context: 776 BCE is the traditional date. Held every four years at Olympia until abolished by Roman Emperor Theodosius I in 393 CE for being a pagan festival. Revived in 1896.

question: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built for which pharaoh, and roughly when?
context: Built around 2560 BCE as Khufu's tomb. Originally about 146.6m tall (now 138.5m after losing its outer casing). Was the tallest human-made structure for about 3,800 years until Lincoln Cathedral (~1311).

question: Where was Napoleon finally exiled after his defeat at Waterloo?
answer: Saint Helena (a remote island in the South Atlantic, where he died in 1821)
context: Exiled to Saint Helena in October 1815. The island is about 1,900 km from the nearest coast — chosen for its remoteness. Napoleon died there in May 1821, possibly of stomach cancer (or, per some theories, arsenic poisoning).

question: Which 1494 treaty divided the newly discovered lands outside Europe between Spain and Portugal?
context: Mediated by Pope Alexander VI. Drew a line down the Atlantic — everything west to Spain, east to Portugal. Why Brazil speaks Portuguese while the rest of South America speaks Spanish.

question: In which year did Thomas Edison patent his practical incandescent light bulb?
context: Patented his carbon-filament bulb in 1879. Joseph Swan had patented a similar design in England around the same time; they eventually merged companies. Edison's real contribution was as much the whole electrical system as the bulb itself.

question: The Dutch Golden Age is most closely associated with which century?
context: Roughly 1588–1672. Coincided with the Eighty Years' War and early Dutch Republic. Featured Rembrandt, Vermeer, Hals, Spinoza, Huygens, the VOC, and Amsterdam as Europe's commercial hub.

question: In which year was slavery formally abolished throughout the United States via the 13th Amendment?
context: 13th Amendment ratified on 6 December 1865. Freed roughly 4 million enslaved people. Lincoln had already been assassinated in April. De facto racial subjugation continued via Jim Crow laws for nearly a century more.

question: Which continent has the most countries?
answer: Africa (54 sovereign states recognised by the UN)
context: 54 UN-recognised sovereign states. Asia has 49. Africa's count grew substantially after the 1960s wave of decolonisation. South Sudan (2011) is the newest African state.

question: What is the capital of Switzerland?
context: Federal City since 1848 — deliberately chosen over Zurich or Geneva to avoid favouring the German- or French-speaking regions. Not formally declared 'capital' in the constitution.

question: What is the capital of Belgium?
answer: Brussels (also the de facto capital of the EU)
context: Home to most EU institutions (European Commission, Council, much of Parliament). Officially bilingual (French and Dutch) — and trilingual if you add the sizeable English-speaking expat population.

question: What is the capital of Turkey?
context: Made the capital by Atatürk in 1923, replacing Istanbul. Part of the deliberate move away from Ottoman symbols and towards a new Anatolian identity.

question: What is the capital of Egypt?
context: Largest city in the Arab world and Africa — metropolitan population about 22 million. Site of the Pyramids of Giza just across the Nile.

question: What is the capital of Nigeria?
context: Purpose-built capital, replacing Lagos in 1991. Centrally located and ethnically neutral between the country's major regions. Lagos remains the largest city and economic hub.

question: What is the capital of Argentina?
context: Name means 'good airs' in Spanish. Metropolitan area about 15 million — home to roughly a third of Argentina's population.

question: Which country has the largest land area in Africa?
answer: Algeria (~2.38 million km², after South Sudan's independence reduced Sudan)
context: About 2.38 million km². Became Africa's largest by land area when South Sudan gained independence in 2011, reducing Sudan.

question: Which mountain range is traditionally considered the border between Europe and Asia?
answer: The Ural Mountains (along with the Ural River and the Caucasus)
context: The Ural Mountains plus the Ural River and the Caucasus form the traditional dividing line. Entirely a cultural and historical convention — geologically Europe and Asia are one continent (Eurasia).

question: What is the longest mountain range above sea level?
answer: The Andes (~7,000 km, along the western edge of South America)
context: About 7,000 km long. The longer Mid-Atlantic Ridge is larger overall but mostly underwater. Andes contains Aconcagua (6,961m), the highest peak outside Asia.

question: What is the highest peak in the Alps?
answer: Mont Blanc (~4,808m, on the French-Italian border)
context: Mont Blanc, about 4,808m. On the French–Italian border; Chamonix on the French side is a major climbing base. Exact height varies by a few metres with snowpack.

question: Which volcano destroyed Pompeii and Herculaneum in 79 CE?
context: Erupted in October 79 CE (traditionally August, now revised). Buried Pompeii under ash and Herculaneum under pyroclastic flow. Still an active volcano today — roughly 3 million people now live nearby.

question: In which year did the Panama Canal officially open?
context: Opened August 1914 after about 10 years of construction. Around 30,000 workers died (most from disease). Transferred from US to Panamanian control in 1999.

question: In which year did the Suez Canal open?
context: Opened November 1869 after 10 years of construction. Connects the Mediterranean and Red Sea. Nationalised by Egypt in 1956, triggering the Suez Crisis.

question: Which river flows through Paris?
context: About 777 km long. The 1910 flood crested 8.62m above normal — still the benchmark. Its banks are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: Which river flows through Rome?
context: About 406 km long. Rome was traditionally founded on its eastern bank in 753 BCE. Famous Tiber Island is the only island in the city.

question: What is the approximate height of Mount Fuji, Japan's highest mountain?
context: 3,776m. Sacred in Shinto tradition. An active volcano, last erupted 1707. UNESCO World Heritage site since 2013 — for cultural rather than natural reasons.

question: The Equator passes through how many countries?
answer: 13 (depending on how you count Kiribati's islands and the Galápagos)
context: 13 countries: São Tomé and Príncipe, Gabon, Republic of the Congo, DRC, Uganda, Kenya, Somalia, Indonesia, Kiribati, Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil (and sometimes the Maldives or the US are included depending on counting).

question: Which lake holds the largest volume of fresh water on Earth?
answer: Lake Baikal in Siberia (about 23% of all fresh surface water)
context: Holds about 23% of all unfrozen surface freshwater on Earth. Deeper, older and more voluminous than any of the North American Great Lakes combined.

question: What is the capital of Iceland?
context: Population about 140,000 — the northernmost capital of a sovereign state. 'Reykjavík' means 'smoky bay', after the steam from nearby hot springs.

question: What is the capital of Finland?
context: Population about 680,000. Founded 1550 by Sweden's King Gustav Vasa. The core city is built on a peninsula, with dozens of surrounding islands.

question: What is the capital of Portugal?
context: Oldest capital in Western Europe after Athens — over 2,700 years of continuous habitation. Destroyed by the catastrophic 1755 earthquake and rebuilt in a rigid grid.

question: What is the capital of Ireland?
context: Population about 550,000 (Greater Dublin ~2 million). Capital of the Republic since 1922; before that the second city of the British Empire.

question: Venice is built on approximately how many small islands?
context: Built on roughly 118 small islands in a lagoon, connected by over 400 bridges. Sinking by about 1–2mm per year. The MOSE flood barrier system was fully activated in 2020.

question: Addis Ababa is the capital of which country?
context: Population about 5 million. Founded 1886. Seat of the African Union. Name means 'new flower' in Amharic.

question: Einstein's equation E=mc² was introduced in which of his theories?
context: Part of Special Relativity (1905), not General Relativity (1915). Shows that mass and energy are interconvertible. Underlies both nuclear power and nuclear weapons.

question: In which month and year was the James Webb Space Telescope launched?
context: Launched 25 December 2021 from French Guiana. Orbits the Sun at the L2 Lagrange point, about 1.5 million km from Earth. Successor to Hubble, operates primarily in infrared.

question: In which year was the Hubble Space Telescope launched?
context: Launched by Space Shuttle Discovery on 24 April 1990. Its initial primary mirror was famously flawed — corrective optics installed in 1993. Still operational.

question: Who proposed the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953?
answer: James Watson and Francis Crick (with crucial X-ray diffraction data from Rosalind Franklin)
context: Published in Nature, 25 April 1953. Relied on Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray diffraction image, shown to them without her knowledge. She died of ovarian cancer in 1958; Nobels aren't awarded posthumously.

question: How many different nucleotide bases are there in DNA?
answer: 4 (adenine, thymine, guanine, cytosine — A, T, G, C)
context: Adenine, thymine, guanine, cytosine. A pairs with T, G with C — Chargaff's rule. RNA uses uracil (U) instead of thymine.

question: Who first organised the periodic table in 1869?
context: Mendeleev organised elements by atomic weight and periodic properties, leaving gaps that correctly predicted undiscovered elements. Element 101 was named mendelevium in his honour.

question: What is the most abundant element in the Earth's crust?
context: Oxygen (~46% by mass), followed by silicon (~28%), aluminium (~8%) and iron (~5%). Most of it is locked in silicate rocks and oxides, not free.

question: What is the most abundant element in the universe?
context: Roughly 75% of normal matter by mass. Fuses into helium in stars. Dark matter (which isn't made of ordinary elements) actually outweighs all visible matter several times over.

question: What is the acronym for the colours of the visible light spectrum, in order?
answer: ROYGBIV (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet)
context: Newton originally proposed seven colours — partly because seven matched the musical scale. Modern physics often omits indigo as a distinct band.

question: What are the three classical states of matter?
answer: Solid, liquid, and gas (plasma is often counted as a fourth)
context: Plasma is usually counted as the fourth. More exotic states — Bose-Einstein condensates, degenerate matter — exist only at extreme temperatures or pressures.

question: How many chambers does the human heart have?
context: Two atria (receiving chambers) and two ventricles (pumping chambers). Beats roughly 100,000 times a day, pumping about 7,500 litres of blood.

question: What is the longest bone in the human body?
context: The thigh bone. Typically about 48cm in an adult (roughly a quarter of total height). Can support up to 30 times body weight.

question: Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell, was born in which year?
context: Born 5 July 1996 at the Roslin Institute, Scotland. Cloned from a mammary-gland cell — hence the name, after Dolly Parton. Died 2003, aged 6 (normal lifespan is ~12).

question: Roughly how long ago did the non-avian dinosaurs go extinct?
answer: About 66 million years ago (end of the Cretaceous, likely triggered by the Chicxulub asteroid impact)
context: About 66 million years ago, at the end of the Cretaceous. Almost certainly triggered by the Chicxulub asteroid impact off the Yucatán. Birds are surviving dinosaurs.

question: Who proposed the theory of continental drift in 1912?
context: German meteorologist. His idea was rejected for decades because he couldn't propose a plausible mechanism. Plate tectonics (1960s) finally confirmed and explained it.

question: What name is given to the supercontinent from which today's continents drifted apart?
context: Pangaea (from Greek: 'all Earth') existed around 335–175 million years ago. Before Pangaea there were earlier supercontinents (Rodinia, Columbia). A new one — 'Pangaea Ultima' — is predicted in about 250 million years.

question: What physical phenomenon causes the sky to appear blue?
answer: Rayleigh scattering (shorter wavelengths of sunlight scatter more in the atmosphere)
context: Rayleigh scattering: sunlight's shorter (blue) wavelengths scatter more strongly through air molecules than longer (red) ones. At sunset the light travels further through the atmosphere, so more blue scatters away and red dominates.

question: What are the Sun's two main elements by mass?
context: Hydrogen (~74%) and helium (~24%). Helium was actually detected in the Sun via spectroscopy (1868) before being found on Earth — hence the name from Greek 'helios' (Sun).

question: Who is considered the founder of psychoanalysis?
context: Austrian neurologist, 1856–1939. Foundational theorist of psychoanalysis, though much of his specific theory (Oedipus complex, penis envy, etc.) is now largely rejected in clinical psychology. His cultural influence remains enormous.

question: Which Russian physiologist is famous for demonstrating classical conditioning in dogs?
context: Won the 1904 Nobel for work on the digestive system — the famous salivating-dog experiments came afterwards. 'Classical conditioning' became foundational in behaviourism.

question: What is the approximate value of the golden ratio (φ)?
context: Approximately 1.61803398... Appears in certain plant growth patterns, spiral shells, and artistic composition. Its role in art and nature is often overclaimed.

question: Who developed the first successful smallpox vaccine in 1796?
context: English physician. Noticed milkmaids who'd had cowpox didn't catch smallpox. 'Vaccine' comes from the Latin 'vacca' (cow). Smallpox was eradicated globally in 1980 — the only human disease ever eradicated.

question: Who is credited with inventing the telephone in 1876?
context: Scottish-born, worked in the US and Canada. Patent awarded 1876; priority disputed with Elisha Gray, who filed a similar design on the same day. Bell Labs remains named after him.

question: Who discovered radioactivity in 1896?
answer: Henri Becquerel (later shared the 1903 Nobel with the Curies)
context: Discovered that uranium salts emitted a penetrating radiation independent of external stimulus. Shared the 1903 Nobel Physics Prize with Pierre and Marie Curie.

question: Who is credited with pioneering long-distance radio transmission in the 1890s?
context: Italian engineer. Sent the first transatlantic wireless signal in 1901. Shared the 1909 Nobel in Physics. His pioneering work was essential to modern radio, though parallel work by Tesla and others also contributed.

question: Who wrote "Great Expectations"?
context: Published 1861 serialised. Narrator Pip's moral education is one of the great bildungsromans in English. The ending was rewritten after Dickens's friend Bulwer-Lytton called the original too bleak.

question: Who wrote "Jane Eyre"?
context: Published 1847 under the pseudonym 'Currer Bell'. Pioneering first-person female narrative. 'Reader, I married him' is one of English literature's most famous lines.

question: Who wrote "Wuthering Heights"?
context: Published 1847 under 'Ellis Bell'. Emily's only novel — she died the following year aged 30. Initial reviews were hostile; the book's reputation grew posthumously.

question: Who wrote "Mrs Dalloway"?
context: Published 1925. One day in the life of Clarissa Dalloway in post-WWI London. Pioneered stream of consciousness in English alongside Joyce and Faulkner.

question: Who wrote "Gone with the Wind"?
context: Published 1936, Pulitzer 1937. Sold roughly 30 million copies. The 1939 film adaptation won 10 Oscars — a record at the time. Heavily criticised in recent decades for its portrayal of slavery.

question: Who wrote "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea"?
context: French, 1828–1905. 'Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas' (1869–70) introduced Captain Nemo and the Nautilus. One of the most translated authors of all time — sometimes ranked second after Agatha Christie.

question: Who wrote "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland"?
answer: Lewis Carroll (the pen name of Charles Lutwidge Dodgson)
context: Real name Charles Lutwidge Dodgson. Published 1865. Dodgson was also an Oxford mathematician — the Mad Tea-Party scene is partly a maths joke. Told the story first to young Alice Liddell on a 1862 boat trip.

question: Who created Peter Pan?
context: Scottish playwright. Peter first appeared in the 1902 novel 'The Little White Bird'; the stage play came in 1904. Barrie bequeathed the rights to Great Ormond Street Hospital, which still benefits.

question: Who wrote the Harry Potter novels?
context: Born 1965. Wrote the first Harry Potter manuscript largely in Edinburgh cafés while on benefits as a single mother. Seven books (1997–2007). Total sales over 600 million copies in 80+ languages.

question: In which year was the first Harry Potter book published in the UK?
context: Published 26 June 1997 by Bloomsbury after a dozen other publishers had rejected it. Initial print run was just 500 copies. Titled 'Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone' in the US.

question: Who wrote "Catch-22"?
context: Published 1961. Satire of military bureaucracy — the titular 'catch' is that you have to be crazy to be discharged, but asking to be discharged proves you're sane. The phrase entered everyday English.

question: Who wrote "Fahrenheit 451"?
context: Published 1953. Title refers to the temperature at which book paper ignites. Firemen burn books rather than fight fires. Bradbury denied it was about censorship — said it was about TV dumbing people down.

question: Who wrote "Slaughterhouse-Five"?
context: Published 1969. Based on Vonnegut's real experience surviving the Allied firebombing of Dresden as a POW in 1945. 'So it goes.'

question: Who wrote the sci-fi novel "Dune" (1965)?
context: Published 1965 after being rejected by 20+ publishers. Now considered one of the greatest science fiction novels. Denis Villeneuve's recent two-film adaptation (2021, 2024) brought it back to mass audiences.

question: Who wrote "The Kite Runner"?
context: Afghan-American, published 2003. About two boys — one Pashtun, one Hazara — in Kabul before and during the Soviet invasion and Taliban rule. Hosseini is also a physician.

question: Who wrote "Rebecca" (1938)?
context: Published 1938. Gothic thriller whose unnamed narrator marries a widower haunted by his first wife. Hitchcock's 1940 adaptation won Best Picture. Opening line: 'Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again.'

question: Who wrote "A Song of Ice and Fire," the book series adapted into Game of Thrones?
context: Five books published 1996–2011. The TV series overtook the books in 2016. Martin has been working on 'The Winds of Winter' for over a decade — publication still unannounced.

question: Who wrote "The War of the Worlds"?
context: Published 1898 as magazine serial, then book. One of the earliest and most influential alien-invasion stories. Orson Welles's 1938 radio adaptation famously caused some listeners to believe an actual Martian invasion was happening.

question: Who wrote "Heart of Darkness"?
context: Published 1899. Based on Conrad's own 1890 experience as a steamboat captain on the Congo. Foundational text of modernist fiction; loose basis for 'Apocalypse Now'. Its racial politics have been intensely debated since Chinua Achebe's 1977 critique.

question: Who wrote "Lord of the Flies"?
context: Published 1954. Won the 1983 Nobel. Allegory of civilisation's collapse — British schoolboys marooned on an island descend into savagery. Written partly in response to the optimistic children's adventure stories of Golding's youth.

question: Who is the traditional author of the Iliad and the Odyssey?
context: Homer is a traditional name — the modern scholarly consensus is that the Iliad and Odyssey are products of oral tradition compiled over centuries, probably 8th century BCE. Whether 'Homer' was one person or several is still debated.

question: Which ancient Greek playwright wrote "Oedipus Rex"?
context: Part of Sophocles's Theban trilogy (with Oedipus at Colonus and Antigone). Freud famously used the Oedipus myth for his theory of the Oedipus complex. Premiered around 429 BCE in Athens.

question: Who wrote "Death in Venice" (Der Tod in Venedig)?
context: Published 1912. An ageing German writer becomes obsessed with a beautiful Polish boy in cholera-stricken Venice. Mann won the Nobel in 1929. Visconti's 1971 film set it to Mahler's 5th Symphony.

question: Which Dutch novelist wrote "De Avonden" (The Evenings, 1947)?
context: Published 1947. Ten-day diary of a young Amsterdammer adrift in the post-war emptiness. Long considered the defining Dutch post-war novel. Reve later became notorious for eccentric public provocations.

question: Which Dutch author won the 2020 International Booker Prize for "The Discomfort of Evening"?
context: Rijneveld is non-binary (uses 'they/them'). Won the International Booker 2020 at age 28 — youngest-ever winner. The novel follows a farming family after the death of a child. Rijneveld is also a celebrated poet.

question: Who holds the record for most Best Actress Oscar wins, with four?
context: 4 Oscars from 12 nominations (1933, 1967, 1968, 1981). Her last win was for 'On Golden Pond' at age 74.

question: Which film was Pixar's first feature, released in 1995?
context: First entirely computer-animated feature film. Directed by John Lasseter. Pixar's partnership with Disney led to Disney acquiring Pixar in 2006 for $7.4 billion.

question: In which year was The Walt Disney Company founded?
context: Founded October 1923 by Walt and Roy Disney as a cartoon studio. Now one of the world's largest entertainment conglomerates (Marvel, Pixar, Lucasfilm, 21st Century Fox, ESPN).

question: In which year did Mickey Mouse make his cinema debut in "Steamboat Willie"?
context: Released 18 November 1928 — celebrated as Mickey's official birthday. Not actually the first Mickey cartoon produced, but the first with synchronised sound.

question: Which 1960 Alfred Hitchcock film features the famous shower scene?
context: The shower scene took a week to shoot with 70+ camera angles for a 45-second sequence. Bernard Herrmann's shrieking violins became iconic. Hitchcock killed the lead (Janet Leigh) a third of the way through — unprecedented at the time.

question: Which Japanese director made "Seven Samurai" (1954)?
context: 1910–1998. Heavily influenced by American Westerns; his films in turn influenced Hollywood. 'Seven Samurai' was remade as 'The Magnificent Seven'; 'Yojimbo' as 'A Fistful of Dollars'; 'The Hidden Fortress' directly inspired Star Wars.

question: Which Italian director made "La Dolce Vita" and "8½"?
context: 1920–1993. 'La Dolce Vita' (1960) gave us the word 'paparazzi' (from a character name). '8½' (1963) is a metafictional masterpiece about a director with director's block.

question: Of what nationality was the director Ingmar Bergman?
context: 1918–2007. Made over 60 films in a 60-year career. 'The Seventh Seal' (1957), 'Persona' (1966), 'Fanny and Alexander' (1982). Died on Fårö, Sweden — the same day as Michelangelo Antonioni.

question: Which film won Best Picture at the 2024 Oscars?
context: 7 Oscars including Best Director for Nolan. Based on Kai Bird and Martin J. Sherwin's biography 'American Prometheus'. Part of the 'Barbenheimer' phenomenon with Greta Gerwig's 'Barbie' opening the same weekend.

question: Which film won Best Picture at the 2025 Oscars?
context: Sean Baker won Best Director, Best Original Screenplay, Best Film Editing, and Best Picture — a modern record for a single film by one director. Mikey Madison won Best Actress.

question: Which film won Best Picture at the 2023 Oscars?
context: Directed by Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert ('Daniels'). Won 7 Oscars. First film with an Asian lead actress (Michelle Yeoh) to win Best Picture. Only the second sci-fi film to win Best Picture.

question: How many symphonies did Beethoven complete?
context: 9 complete symphonies. The 10th was in sketches when he died. The 9th premiered in 1824 — four years before his death. Also completed 32 piano sonatas, 5 piano concertos, and 1 violin concerto.

question: Of what nationality was the Impressionist composer Claude Debussy?
context: 1862–1918. 'Clair de Lune' (1890), 'La Mer' (1905), 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune' (1894). Influenced by Javanese gamelan he heard at the 1889 Paris World's Fair.

question: Of what nationality was the composer Franz Schubert?
context: 1797–1828. Died at 31, likely of syphilis. Composed over 600 lieder (art songs), plus 9 symphonies — the 'Unfinished' Symphony (1822) remains famous.

question: In which city is the famous opera house La Scala?
context: Opened 1778. Known for hostile claques who would boo and whistle at singers they disliked. Central to Italian opera tradition for over two centuries.

question: In which city is the Metropolitan Opera?
context: In Lincoln Center. The current opera house opened 1966 — the original building dated from 1883. Hosts the famous 'Live in HD' cinema broadcasts since 2006.

question: In which Dutch city is the Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra based?
context: Concertgebouw ('concert building') in Amsterdam. Opened 1888. Acoustics consistently rated among the world's best alongside the Vienna Musikverein and Boston Symphony Hall.

question: What was Luciano Pavarotti's vocal range?
answer: Tenor (one of the most celebrated operatic tenors of the 20th century)
context: 1935–2007. 'The Three Tenors' phenomenon (with Domingo and Carreras, from 1990) brought opera to a mass audience. His rendition of 'Nessun dorma' at the 1990 World Cup is iconic.

question: Maria Callas was famous for what kind of voice?
answer: Soprano (Greek-American, one of the most renowned opera singers of the 20th century)
context: 1923–1977. Greek-American. Famous for her dramatic performances and the bel canto revival. Tumultuous private life — including an affair with Aristotle Onassis.

question: Which Korean pop group broke into the global mainstream in the 2010s with songs like "Dynamite" and "Butter"?
context: Seven members. Debuted 2013. First Korean act to reach #1 on the Billboard 200. Completed mandatory military service 2023–2025 and resumed group activity in 2026.

question: In which US city is jazz generally considered to have originated around 1900?
context: New Orleans, around 1900. Born of a mix of African, Caribbean, and European musical traditions. Storyville (the legal red-light district) was a key early venue before its closure in 1917.

question: What instrument was Miles Davis most famous for playing?
context: 1926–1991. 'Kind of Blue' (1959) is the best-selling jazz album ever. Constantly reinvented his sound — bebop, cool jazz, modal, fusion.

question: Who composed the piano piece "Clair de Lune"?
context: Part of his 'Suite bergamasque' (1890, published 1905). Title means 'moonlight' in French — inspired by Paul Verlaine's poem.

question: Which Dutch DJ, born Tijs Verwest, became one of the world's best-known trance/electronic producers?
context: Born in Breda in 1969. 'Adagio for Strings' (2005) and 'Traffic' (2003) were defining tracks of trance. Later pivoted to more mainstream EDM/pop.

question: Who composed "The Blue Danube" waltz?
context: Premiered 1866 in Vienna after initial poor reception. Became Austria's unofficial second anthem. Features prominently in Kubrick's '2001: A Space Odyssey' (1968).

question: Which Italian Baroque painter is associated with dramatic chiaroscuro (tenebrism)?
context: 1571–1610. Pioneered dramatic light-dark contrasts. Killed a man in a brawl in Rome, fled, lived as a fugitive, died mysteriously on a beach. Rediscovered in the 20th century — neglected for centuries.

question: Who painted "The Birth of Venus" (c. 1485)?
context: Around 1485, tempera on canvas. In the Uffizi, Florence. Venus stands on a scallop shell; model was probably Simonetta Vespucci, a Florentine beauty. Commissioned by the Medici family.

question: Who painted "The School of Athens" (1509–11, in the Vatican)?
context: Fresco in the Vatican's Stanza della Segnatura, 1509–11. Depicts Plato, Aristotle, Socrates, Pythagoras, Euclid and others — with Plato's face modelled on Leonardo and Heraclitus on Michelangelo.

question: Who founded the Bauhaus school in 1919?
context: Modernist design school founded in Weimar, 1919. Closed by the Nazis in 1933. Principles of 'form follows function' shaped modern design worldwide. Tutors included Klee, Kandinsky, Mies van der Rohe.

question: Which Russian avant-garde artist painted "Black Square" (1915)?
context: 1879–1935. 'Black Square' was exhibited in 1915 as the 'zero point of painting' — a conscious rejection of representation. Founder of Suprematism. Later pressured under Stalin to return to figurative work.

question: Wassily Kandinsky is regarded as a pioneer of what kind of art?
context: 1866–1944. Russian-born, trained as a lawyer. Claimed to have experienced synaesthesia — seeing colours when hearing music. His 1910 'Composition VII' is often cited as one of the first purely abstract paintings.

question: Which art movement is most associated with Henri Matisse?
context: 1869–1954. Fauvism (1905–08) featured wild, unrealistic colours ('fauves' means 'wild beasts'). Later turned to cut-paper collages in old age after surgery left him unable to paint standing.

question: Edgar Degas is most famous for paintings of which subject?
context: 1834–1917. About half of his ~1,500 works feature dancers. Considered himself a Realist, not an Impressionist, despite exhibiting with the group. Failing eyesight pushed him from painting to sculpture and pastels.

question: Which Spanish Romantic painter made "The Third of May 1808"?
context: 1746–1828. 'The Third of May 1808' (1814) depicts Spanish civilians being executed by Napoleonic troops. His dark later 'Black Paintings' (1819–23) anticipated Expressionism by a century.

question: Who painted "American Gothic" (1930)?
context: 1891–1942. Depicts a farmer and his daughter (painted as Wood's sister and his dentist) standing before a small Gothic-Revival house in Iowa. Instantly iconic — one of the most parodied paintings in American culture.

question: Which Dada artist exhibited a urinal titled "Fountain" in 1917?
context: 1887–1968. 'Fountain' — a porcelain urinal signed 'R. Mutt' — was rejected from a 1917 exhibition. Became a cornerstone of conceptual art. Duchamp spent decades on his final secret work 'Étant donnés', revealed after his death.

question: In which English county is Stonehenge located?
context: Wiltshire, England. Built in phases from about 3000 BCE to 1600 BCE. Megaliths weighing up to 25 tonnes; the bluestones were transported from Wales (~240 km away). Astronomical alignments with sunrise on summer solstice.

question: What are the massive stone heads on Easter Island called?
context: About 1,000 moai were carved by the Rapa Nui people between roughly 1250 and 1500 CE. Average height about 4m, the largest ~10m and 75 tonnes. Surprisingly, many have full bodies buried below the heads.

question: Whose tomb is guarded by the Terracotta Army in Xi'an?
answer: Qin Shi Huang (first emperor of unified China, d. 210 BCE)
context: Qin Shi Huang, 259–210 BCE. First emperor to unify China. Over 8,000 life-sized terracotta soldiers (each with a different face) plus horses, chariots and officials — meant to protect him in the afterlife. Discovered by accident by local farmers in 1974.

question: The Alhambra palace is in which Spanish city?
context: Granada, Andalusia. Built mostly under the Nasrid dynasty (13th–14th c), the last Muslim kingdom in Iberia. Surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492 — the same year they funded Columbus.

question: The Palace of Versailles was principally built for which French king?
context: Louis XIV (reigned 1643–1715). Transformed his father's hunting lodge into one of Europe's grandest palaces. Court moved there from Paris in 1682. Designed partly to keep nobles under royal surveillance.

question: In which city is the Centre Pompidou?
context: Opened 1977. Designed by Renzo Piano and Richard Rogers with the building's 'guts' — pipes, ducts, escalators — on the outside. Houses the largest modern-art collection in Europe.

question: In which city is the Van Gogh Museum?
context: Opened 1973 on Amsterdam's Museumplein. Houses the largest collection of Van Gogh works in the world — over 200 paintings and 500 drawings. Extension by Kisho Kurokawa added in 1999.

question: In which Dutch city is the Mauritshuis museum?
answer: The Hague (home to Vermeer's "Girl with a Pearl Earring")
context: The Hague. A compact 17th-century nobleman's house turned museum. Houses Vermeer's 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' and 'View of Delft', plus works by Rembrandt, Rubens and Hals.

question: The Kröller-Müller Museum in Otterlo holds the world's second-largest collection of works by which artist?
context: Kröller-Müller Museum holds around 90 paintings and 180 drawings — the world's second-largest Van Gogh collection after the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam. Set in the Hoge Veluwe national park.

question: Who sculpted "Cloud Gate" (nicknamed "The Bean") in Chicago's Millennium Park?
context: Born India 1954, British-based. Stainless-steel sculpture installed 2006. Reflects Chicago's skyline back at viewers. 'The Bean' is now one of the city's defining landmarks.

question: Which contemporary American artist is famous for large balloon-animal sculptures?
context: Born 1955. Balloon Dog (Orange) sold for $58.4 million in 2013 — then the record for a living artist at auction. His work polarises critics — celebrated as pop-art brilliance or dismissed as kitsch.

question: Of what nationality was the Renaissance artist Albrecht Dürer?
context: 1471–1528. From Nuremberg. His woodcuts and engravings ('Knight, Death and the Devil', 'Melencolia I') are among the most technically accomplished ever made. Self-portraits were unusually introspective for his time.

question: Which German Romantic painter made "Wanderer above the Sea of Fog"?
context: 1774–1840. The Romantic landscape painter par excellence — solitary figures contemplating vast natural scenes. 'Wanderer above the Sea of Fog' (1818) is among the most recognisable Romantic paintings.

question: El Greco (born in Crete) spent most of his career working in which country?
context: 1541–1614. Born Doménikos Theotokópoulos in Crete. Settled in Toledo from 1577. Distinctive elongated figures and dramatic colour — centuries ahead of his time; only properly rediscovered around 1900.

question: How many players does a rugby union team field?
context: 15 players (rugby league uses 13). Rugby union split from association football in 1863 and from rugby league in 1895.

question: How many players does an American football team have on the field per side?
context: 11 on the field per side, but with unlimited substitutions and entirely separate offensive and defensive squads — so active roster sizes are around 53 players per team.

question: How many players are on a baseball team's fielding lineup?
context: 9 fielders: pitcher, catcher, 1B, 2B, 3B, SS, LF, CF, RF. Hitting lineup is also 9, with designated hitter rules varying by league.

question: How many skaters (including the goalie) does each ice hockey team have on the ice?
context: 6 including the goaltender. Teams also field a player short during power plays (the penalty-box penalty is what makes hockey strategy distinctive).

question: What is the highest possible score in a single three-dart throw in darts?
context: 180 is the maximum in a single three-dart throw — three triple 20s. Professional darts scoring starts at 501 and counts down; a '9-dart finish' is the sport's equivalent of a perfect game.

question: What is the maximum break in a single frame of snooker?
context: 147 is achieved by potting all 15 reds with blacks (15 × 8 = 120), then all six colours in sequence (2+3+4+5+6+7 = 27). Ronnie O'Sullivan holds the record for most competitive 147s (15+).

question: Who won the 2024 World Chess Championship, becoming the youngest world champion at 18?
answer: Gukesh Dommaraju (India), defeating defending champion Ding Liren
context: Gukesh became the youngest undisputed world champion at 18, breaking Garry Kasparov's 1985 record (22). Defeated defending champion Ding Liren in Singapore.

question: In which year was the first Formula 1 World Championship race held?
context: First F1 World Championship race: Silverstone, 13 May 1950. Won by Giuseppe Farina (Alfa Romeo). Juan Manuel Fangio won five titles in the 1950s — a record unbroken until Schumacher.

question: What is the Olympic motto (since 2021, in its expanded form)?
answer: "Citius, Altius, Fortius — Communiter" ("Faster, Higher, Stronger — Together")
context: Added 'Communiter' ('Together') in 2021 at the Tokyo Games. Original motto was coined by Father Henri Didon in 1891 and adopted by Coubertin.

question: In which Swiss city is FIFA headquartered?
context: FIFA has been based in Zurich since its founding in 1904. Headquartered at the 'Home of FIFA' building since 2007. Regularly criticised for opaque governance.

question: In which Swiss city is the International Olympic Committee headquartered?
context: IOC based in Lausanne since 1915 (moved from Paris during WWI). The 'Olympic Capital' hosts the Olympic Museum and dozens of international sport federations.

question: Which team won the first European Cup (now Champions League) in 1956?
context: 13 May 1956, Paris. Real Madrid beat Stade de Reims 4–3. Real then won the first five editions (1956–60) — still a record.

question: Who holds the record for most Super Bowl wins as a quarterback?
context: 7 Super Bowl wins — 6 with the New England Patriots, 1 with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers (2021). Retired 2023 at age 45.

question: What is Wayne Gretzky's famous nickname in ice hockey?
context: Born 1961 in Brantford, Ontario. Holds or shares 61 NHL records. Most points in NHL history (2,857) by a wide margin.

question: Who holds the women's record for Grand Slam singles titles, with 24?
context: Australian. Won 24 Grand Slams between 1960 and 1973, including the Calendar Grand Slam in 1970. Serena Williams retired with 23.

question: Who holds the men's pole vault world record as of March 2026?
answer: Armand "Mondo" Duplantis (Sweden) — 6.31m, set in Uppsala in March 2026
context: Set 6.31m in Uppsala, Sweden, in March 2026 — Duplantis's 14th world record. He has dominated men's pole vault since 2020 and is already considered the greatest ever.

question: Who became the NBA's all-time leading scorer in February 2023, passing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar?
context: Passed Kareem's 38,387 points on 7 February 2023. Still extending the record as of 2026 with the Lakers.

question: What does the IOC stand for?
context: Founded 1894 by Pierre de Coubertin. 206 National Olympic Committees are members. Selects Olympic host cities years in advance.

question: Which two players contested the famous 1973 "Battle of the Sexes" tennis match?
answer: Billie Jean King and Bobby Riggs (King won)
context: 20 September 1973 at the Houston Astrodome. King, 29, beat Riggs, 55, in straight sets. Watched by ~90 million worldwide. A milestone for women's sport.
option_c: Billie Jean King and Bobby Riggs
option_d: Billie Jean King and Jimmy Connors

question: Which Dutch speed skater won the Olympic 5000m at three consecutive Winter Games (2010, 2014, 2018)?
context: Won 5000m gold at Vancouver 2010, Sochi 2014 and PyeongChang 2018. Only skater ever to defend the 5000m title three times consecutively.

question: Why is the Dutch national football team known as "Oranje"?
answer: After the House of Orange-Nassau, the royal family of the Netherlands
context: Named for the House of Orange-Nassau — the Dutch royal family descended from William of Orange. Dutch national teams wear orange across most sports despite the country's flag being red/white/blue.
option_c: The orange in the Dutch flag

question: Who has won the most men's Ballon d'Or awards, with 8?
answer: Lionel Messi (as of 2023; the 2024 and 2025 awards went to Rodri and Dembélé)
context: 8 Ballons d'Or: 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2015, 2019, 2021, 2023. Ronaldo has 5 — until recently a fierce rivalry that defined an era.

question: Who set the men's mile world record of 3:43.13 in 1999, a mark still standing?
context: Set at Rome's Golden Gala, 7 July 1999. Still stands as of 2026 — one of athletics' longest-standing records. El Guerrouj also held the 1500m record until 2024.

question: Which Dutch figure skater won Olympic gold at Innsbruck in 1964?
context: Gold in women's figure skating at Innsbruck 1964 — the Netherlands' first-ever Winter Olympic gold outside speed skating. Still a rare success for the country in figure skating.

question: How many players are on a field hockey team (on the field at once)?
answer: 11 (the Netherlands is historically one of the dominant nations in the sport)
context: 11 on field plus substitutes. The Dutch men's and women's teams have been dominant — the women have won 9 World Cups and are historically the top-ranked side.

question: What is the currency of Australia?
context: AUD. Introduced 1966, replacing the Australian pound at a 2:1 ratio. Polymer banknotes since 1988 — Australia pioneered them worldwide.

question: What is the currency of China?
context: Officially 'renminbi' ('people's currency'); the 'yuan' is the main unit. Currency code CNY. Tightly managed by the People's Bank of China.

question: What is the currency of Russia?
context: RUB. 1 ruble = 100 kopeks. Ruble sign (₽) officially introduced in 2013. Heavily devalued after 2022 Western sanctions.

question: What is the currency of Israel?
context: ILS. Introduced 1985, replacing the hyperinflationary old shekel at 1,000:1. The word 'shekel' dates to antiquity — originally a unit of weight for silver.

question: What is the currency of Saudi Arabia?
context: SAR. Pegged to the US dollar since 1986 at a fixed rate of ~3.75 riyals per dollar. Reflects Saudi oil revenues being priced in dollars.

question: What is the official language of Egypt?
context: Modern Standard Arabic for official use; Egyptian Arabic is the spoken dialect — the most widely understood Arabic variety thanks to Egyptian cinema and TV.

question: What is the official language of Greece?
context: Modern Greek. Direct descendant of ancient Greek, albeit significantly evolved. Uses the Greek alphabet — one of the oldest still in use (c. 800 BCE).

question: What is the official language of Iran?
context: Persian (Farsi). Indo-European language, same family as English — despite using Arabic script. Also spoken as Dari in Afghanistan and Tajik in Tajikistan.

question: How many letters are in the modern English alphabet?
context: 26 letters. Old English had additional letters (þ thorn, ð eth, æ ash, ƿ wynn) that dropped out of use in the medieval period.

question: How many letters are in the Spanish alphabet (as officially used)?
context: 27 letters since the Real Academia Española removed 'ch' and 'll' as separate letters in 2010. Only ñ remains uniquely Spanish.

question: What does HTTP stand for?
context: Developed by Tim Berners-Lee and Roy Fielding in the early 1990s. HTTPS (secure version) added TLS encryption. Underlies every standard web transaction.

question: What does HTML stand for?
context: The standard markup language for web pages. Created by Berners-Lee at CERN in 1990. Current version HTML5 (2014).

question: What does URL stand for?
context: Coined by Tim Berners-Lee in the early 1990s. Standardised in RFC 1738 (1994). The structure 'protocol://host/path' is now universal.

question: What do the initials BBC stand for?
context: Founded 1922 by Royal Charter. World's oldest national broadcaster. Funded by the UK TV licence fee. Its World Service broadcasts in over 40 languages.

question: What do the initials IBM stand for?
context: Founded 1911. Ubiquitous from mainframes (1960s) to the personal computer era (IBM PC, 1981). 'Nobody ever got fired for buying IBM' was a mid-20th-century business adage.

question: What is a group of elephants called?
context: A herd is also called a 'parade' or 'memory' (from the idea that elephants remember). Elephant family groups are matriarchal — led by the oldest female.

question: What is a large group of bees in flight called?
answer: A swarm (a stationary colony is a hive)
context: A swarm is in flight; the stationary colony is a hive. Honeybees swarm when the colony divides — the queen leaves with about half the workers.

question: What is a group of sheep called?
context: Sheep in particular don't tend to get special collective nouns in common usage — just 'flock'. A group of lambs is sometimes called a 'fold'.

question: What is the fear of the number 13 called?
context: From Greek 'tris' (three) + 'kai' (and) + 'deka' (ten). 'Paraskevidekatriaphobia' is specifically the fear of Friday the 13th.

question: What does the French phrase "déjà vu" literally mean?
context: Literally 'already seen' in French. The feeling of having previously experienced the current moment. Common and usually harmless, but can be a symptom of temporal-lobe epilepsy.

question: What is the term for a person who draws or makes maps?
context: From Greek 'khartēs' (sheet of papyrus) + '-grapher' (writer). The oldest known world map dates to Babylon, around 600 BCE.

question: What is a person who studies human past through material remains called?
context: From Greek 'arkhaios' (ancient) + '-logia' (study). Distinct from historians in focusing on material remains rather than written sources.

question: Which language has the most non-native speakers in the world?
context: English has more total speakers (~1.5 billion) than any other language, with the majority being non-native (L2). The gap is very wide — Mandarin is next with roughly 200 million L2 speakers.

question: What does "FAQ" stand for?
context: Originates from early internet usage in the 1980s — first documented at the SLAC linear accelerator. Now universal on websites.

question: What is the Dutch word for "windmill"?
context: 'Molen' — from the same Latin root as 'mill' and 'mole' (a unit of chemistry). The Netherlands has about 1,000 surviving historic windmills, down from a peak of ~10,000 in the 19th century.

question: In which year was the Berlin Wall built?
answer: 1961 (the GDR sealed East Berlin on the night of 12–13 August)
context: Built overnight 12–13 August 1961 — initially as barbed wire, then reinforced concrete. Divided Berlin for 28 years. Around 140 people died trying to cross it.

question: In which year did the Russian Revolution take place?
context: Two revolutions in one year. The February Revolution (March by Gregorian calendar) ended Tsarist rule; the October Revolution (November Gregorian) brought Lenin's Bolsheviks to power. Followed by a brutal civil war that ran until 1922.

question: Between which years was the Spanish Civil War fought?
context: Franco's Nationalists (backed by Italy and Germany) against the Republic (backed by the USSR and the International Brigades). Around 500,000 dead. Franco then ruled Spain until 1975.

question: When did Mao Zedong's Cultural Revolution take place in China?
context: Launched by Mao in 1966 to eliminate 'bourgeois' elements. Red Guards persecuted intellectuals; schools and universities closed. Estimates of 1–2 million dead; enormous cultural heritage destroyed. Ended with Mao's death in 1976.

question: In which year did the Tiananmen Square protests culminate in the military crackdown?
context: Student-led pro-democracy protests culminated in military crackdown on the night of 3–4 June 1989. Death toll estimates range from hundreds to several thousand. 'Tank Man' photo became iconic. Topic remains heavily censored in China.

question: In which year did the Louisiana Purchase take place?
answer: 1803 (the US bought ~2.1 million km² from France)
context: Napoleon sold about 2.1 million km² to the US for $15M — around 3 cents per acre. He needed cash for European wars after losing Haiti to a slave revolt. Doubled the size of the US overnight.

question: The Watergate scandal led to the resignation of which US president, and in which year?
context: Resigned 9 August 1974 — the only US president ever to do so. Driven from office by the cover-up of the 1972 Watergate burglary. His successor Gerald Ford pardoned him a month later.

question: In which year was King Charles I of England executed, ending the English Civil War's first phase?
context: Beheaded 30 January 1649 outside Whitehall's Banqueting House — the only English king ever formally executed. Oliver Cromwell led the Commonwealth until the monarchy was restored with Charles II in 1660.

question: What is the date of the Gunpowder Plot — the failed attempt by Guy Fawkes and others to blow up the English Parliament?
context: Catholic conspiracy to blow up Parliament during the State Opening. Discovered when Guy Fawkes was caught in the cellars with 36 barrels of gunpowder. Still commemorated every 5 November with fireworks across the UK.

question: In which year was Mary, Queen of Scots executed?
answer: 1587 (by order of her cousin Elizabeth I)
context: Beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle after 19 years' imprisonment in England. Elizabeth I reluctantly signed the warrant after Catholic plots were tied to Mary. Her son later became James I of England, uniting the crowns.

question: In which year did Hannibal famously cross the Alps into Italy?
context: Crossed with around 50,000 troops and 37 war elephants — most of the elephants died in the snow. Surprised Rome from the north. Won Cannae (216 BCE) but couldn't take Rome itself; eventually defeated at Zama in 202 BCE.

question: In which year was Rome traditionally said to have been founded?
context: Traditional date from Livy. Per legend, Romulus founded the city after killing his brother Remus. Archaeological evidence does show settlement on the Palatine Hill around this period. Romans dated everything 'ab urbe condita' — from the founding of the city.

question: In which year was Socrates executed in Athens?
context: Convicted of 'impiety' and 'corrupting the youth' by an Athenian jury. Chose hemlock over exile. Left no writings — everything known about him comes through Plato and Xenophon.

question: In which years did Mao Zedong's Long March take place?
context: Around 9,000 km retreat of Chinese Communists from Nationalist forces. About 80,000 set out; only about 8,000 completed it. Cemented Mao's leadership and became a foundational myth of Chinese communism.

question: What Dutch term refers to the catastrophic year 1672, when the Republic was attacked by France, England, Münster, and Cologne?
context: Famous contemporary formula: 'het volk redeloos, de regering radeloos, het land reddeloos' — the people irrational, the government desperate, the country lost. Grand Pensionary Johan de Witt was lynched by a mob; William III rose to Stadtholder.

question: Who became King of England after the Glorious Revolution of 1688–89?
answer: William III (Willem III of Orange-Nassau) — jointly with his wife Mary II
context: Dutch Stadtholder invited by English Protestants to overthrow Catholic James II. Landed with a fleet about four times the size of the Spanish Armada. Reigned jointly with his wife Mary. The requirement that the British monarch be Protestant dates from this moment.

question: In which year was Louis XVI of France executed?
context: Guillotined 21 January 1793 at the Place de la Révolution (now Place de la Concorde). Marie Antoinette followed in October. The Revolution then turned on itself in the Reign of Terror.

question: In which year did the Boston Tea Party take place?
context: 16 December 1773. Colonists disguised as Mohawks dumped 342 chests of British tea into Boston Harbor. Protest against 'taxation without representation' and the Tea Act. Only named 'the Tea Party' in the 1820s.

question: The Battles of Lexington and Concord, which began the American Revolutionary War, took place in which year?
context: 19 April 1775. 'The shot heard round the world'. Began the Revolutionary War. Led to the Declaration of Independence the following year.

question: On what date did euro banknotes and coins enter circulation in the eurozone?
context: Coins and notes entered circulation on 1 January 2002 in 12 countries simultaneously. The euro had existed as a virtual accounting currency since 1999. Germany gave up the Deutsche Mark, France the franc, Netherlands the guilder — a huge act of political trust.

question: In which year did the Union of Utrecht lay the foundation for the Dutch Republic?
context: Signed 23 January 1579. Seven northern provinces of the Low Countries formed a defensive alliance against Spanish rule — the foundation of the Dutch Republic. The southern provinces (roughly modern Belgium) remained Catholic and Spanish.

question: The Treaty of Maastricht, which established the European Union, was signed in which year?
context: Signed in Maastricht on 7 February 1992 by the 12 EEC members. Created the European Union as we know it; set out the criteria for monetary union that led to the euro. A founding document you can find celebrated in your current city.

question: In which year was Admiral Horatio Nelson killed at the Battle of Trafalgar?
context: Shot by a French sniper aboard HMS Victory and died hours later. British fleet annihilated the combined French-Spanish fleet off southern Spain. Ended Napoleon's naval threat to Britain. 'England expects that every man will do his duty' became Nelson's final signal.

question: The Storming of the Bastille, now France's national day, occurred on which date?
context: 14 July 1789. Parisians stormed the royal prison-fortress — although only seven prisoners were actually inside. Marked the symbolic start of the French Revolution. Now France's national day.

question: In which year did the UK formally leave the European Union?
answer: 2020 (31 January; the transition period ended 31 December 2020)
context: UK formally left the EU at 23:00 GMT on 31 January 2020 after the 2016 referendum (52–48). The transition period ended 31 December 2020. Only country ever to leave the EU.

question: What is the capital of Poland?
context: Around 90% destroyed by Germans in WWII, particularly after the 1944 Uprising. The Old Town was meticulously rebuilt using 18th-century Canaletto paintings as reference — it's now a UNESCO site.

question: What is the capital of Austria?
context: Capital of the Austro-Hungarian Empire until 1918. Regularly tops world 'most liveable city' rankings. Home to the UN's third-largest office after New York and Geneva.

question: What is the capital of Hungary?
context: Formed 1873 by merging three cities: Buda (west bank, hilly), Pest (east bank, flat), and Óbuda. Split by the Danube. Population about 1.7 million.

question: What is the capital of the Czech Republic?
context: Largely undamaged in WWII, so its medieval core is remarkably preserved. Bohemia/Czech lands became independent from Austria-Hungary in 1918. Velvet Revolution 1989; Czechoslovakia split peacefully into Czech and Slovak republics in 1993.

question: What is the capital of Denmark?
context: 'Merchants' harbour' in Danish. One of the world's most cycle-friendly cities — over 60% of commuters bike daily. Free-city 'Christiania' community has existed since 1971.

question: What is the capital of Ukraine?
answer: Kyiv (preferred transliteration since 2019, replacing Kiev in English usage)
context: Previously Latinised via Russian as 'Kiev' in English until 2019, when Ukraine successfully pushed 'Kyiv' as the standard Romanisation. Founded in the 5th century — the birthplace of Eastern Slavic civilisation (Kyivan Rus).

question: What is the capital of Iraq?
context: Founded 762 CE by Caliph al-Mansur. Was the world's largest city and centre of the Islamic Golden Age. Population around 7 million today.

question: What is the capital of Pakistan?
context: Purpose-built capital inaugurated 1967, replacing Karachi. Closer to the geographic centre of the country. Relatively young and tightly planned.

question: What is the capital of Kenya?
context: Named after a Maasai watering hole. Founded by the British in 1899 as a railway depot. Population about 5 million. Nairobi National Park sits within city limits — unique for a major capital.

question: What is the capital of Cuba?
context: Founded 1519 by Spanish settlers. One of the most important ports in the colonial Americas. Historic centre is UNESCO-listed.

question: What is the capital of Colombia?
context: Sits at around 2,640m — one of the world's highest capitals. Population about 8 million. Founded 1538 by Spanish conquistador Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada.

question: What are the two capitals of Bolivia?
answer: La Paz (administrative / seat of government) and Sucre (constitutional / judicial)
context: La Paz is the administrative/de facto capital and the world's highest de facto capital at ~3,600m. Sucre is the constitutional capital and seat of the judiciary. Unusual two-capital arrangement.

question: What is the capital of North Korea?
context: Means 'flat land' in Korean. One of the world's most closed-off cities. Population around 3 million — mostly regime-loyal citizens with privileged access.

question: What is the capital of Mongolia?
context: Means 'red hero' in Mongolian. Population about 1.5 million — nearly half of Mongolia's entire population lives in the capital.

question: Name the five North American Great Lakes.
answer: Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario (mnemonic: HOMES)
context: HOMES: Huron, Ontario, Michigan, Erie, Superior. Collectively hold about 21% of the world's surface fresh water.
option_c: Great Bear, Superior, Huron, Erie, Ontario

question: Which of the Great Lakes is located entirely within the United States?
context: The only Great Lake entirely within the US. The other four are shared with Canada.

question: What is the longest river in Europe?
answer: The Volga (~3,530 km, entirely in Russia)
context: About 3,530 km. Drains to the Caspian Sea rather than an ocean. Cultural heart of Russia — 'Mother Volga' in folk tradition.

question: What is the largest country in South America?
context: About 8.5 million km² — fifth-largest country in the world. Around 215 million people. Only Portuguese-speaking country in South America, a legacy of the 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas.

question: Which three countries make up the Benelux?
