context: Recorded 1959. Landmark of modal jazz. Featured Bill Evans (piano), John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley (saxophones). Best-selling jazz album of all time — about 5 million copies sold in the US alone.

question: Which 1977 Fleetwood Mac album contains "Go Your Own Way" and "Dreams"?
context: Recorded 1977 amid two break-ups within the band (Buckingham/Nicks and Christine/John McVie) plus Mick Fleetwood's divorce. Sold around 40 million copies. The peak soft-rock album.

question: In which city is Hagia Sophia located?
answer: Istanbul (originally a Byzantine cathedral, later a mosque, later a museum, now a mosque again)
context: Built in 537 CE by Emperor Justinian as the greatest church in Christendom. Became a mosque under the Ottomans (1453). Kemalist Turkey secularised it as a museum in 1934. Erdoğan reconverted it to a mosque in 2020.

question: Angkor Wat — the world's largest religious monument — is in which country?
context: Built in the early 12th century. Originally Hindu (dedicated to Vishnu), later Buddhist. Cambodian national symbol — appears on the flag. The 400 km² UNESCO site is the world's largest religious monument by area.

question: In which country is Machu Picchu?
context: Built around 1450, abandoned about 1572 after the Spanish conquest. The Spanish never actually found it. Rediscovered by American historian Hiram Bingham in 1911. Now draws about 1 million visitors a year — causing significant preservation concerns.

question: The ancient rock-cut city of Petra is in which country?
context: Carved into sandstone cliffs by the Nabataeans from around the 5th century BCE. Abandoned by the 7th century CE. Rediscovered by Western travellers in 1812. Featured famously in Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade (1989).

question: In which country is the Maya pyramid complex Chichen Itza?
context: Main pyramid is 'El Castillo' (Temple of Kukulkan). Built roughly 900–1200 CE. Famous for a shadow-serpent effect that appears along its side at the equinoxes. One of the New 7 Wonders of the World (2007).

question: Who designed the Sydney Opera House, completed in 1973?
context: Danish architect. Won the 1957 design competition. Completed 1973 after massive cost overruns. Utzon resigned in 1966 during construction disputes and never saw the finished building in person. UNESCO site since 2007.

question: Which four US presidents are depicted on Mount Rushmore?
answer: George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Abraham Lincoln, and Theodore Roosevelt
context: Carved 1927–1941 by Gutzon Borglum. 18m-high faces of Washington, Jefferson, Lincoln and Theodore Roosevelt. Located in South Dakota's Black Hills — Lakota Sioux sacred land taken in violation of the 1868 Treaty of Fort Laramie. Controversial for that reason.
option_a: Washington, Adams, Lincoln, and Theodore Roosevelt
option_b: Washington, Lincoln, Jefferson, and Franklin D. Roosevelt
option_c: Washington, Jefferson, Lincoln, and Theodore Roosevelt

question: In which Italian city is the Leaning Tower?
answer: Pisa (it is the bell tower/campanile of Pisa Cathedral)
context: Construction began 1173. The tilt started during construction due to unstable ground. 56m tall, about 4° lean (after 1990s stabilisation; was about 5.5°). 294 steps to the top.

question: In which city is the sail-shaped Burj al Arab hotel?
context: 321m tall. Completed 1999. Sits on an artificial island off Dubai's Jumeirah coast. One of the most expensive hotels in the world. The sail-shaped design is by British architect Tom Wright.

question: What is the official colour of the Golden Gate Bridge?
context: The colour is 'International Orange' — designed to complement the natural landscape and to improve visibility in fog. Completed 1937. 2.7 km long.

question: What is technically "Big Ben" at the Palace of Westminster?
answer: The Great Bell inside the Elizabeth Tower (the tower and clock are commonly but inaccurately called "Big Ben")
context: Technically the name of the 13.7-tonne bell inside Elizabeth Tower (renamed 2012 for the Queen's Diamond Jubilee). The bell began ringing in 1859. Tower refurbished 2017–2022 (around £80 million).
option_b: The Elizabeth Tower as a whole
option_c: The Great Bell inside the Elizabeth Tower

question: In which city is the Brandenburg Gate?
context: Built 1788–1791. Survived WWII. Became a symbol of Cold War division and then of reunification after 9 November 1989. Stands at the end of Unter den Linden.

question: In which city is the Arc de Triomphe?
answer: Paris (at the western end of the Champs-Élysées)
context: Commissioned by Napoleon in 1806, completed 1836. Honours WWI soldiers — the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier with eternal flame lies beneath it. Centre of the Place de l'Étoile, from which twelve avenues radiate.

question: Which 1936 photograph by Dorothea Lange became an icon of the Great Depression?
context: Taken February 1936 at Nipomo, California. Subject was Florence Owens Thompson, a Cherokee woman with seven children. Defining image of the Dust Bowl era. Lange was working for the Farm Security Administration.

question: Which Japanese contemporary artist is world-famous for her polka-dot motifs and mirrored "Infinity Rooms"?
context: Born 1929. Has lived voluntarily in a Tokyo psychiatric hospital since 1977, continuing to work from there. Infinity Rooms, pumpkin sculptures, polka dots. One of the most successful living artists.

question: Which Chinese artist-activist designed (with Herzog & de Meuron) the Beijing "Bird's Nest" stadium consultancy, and is known for installations like "Sunflower Seeds"?
context: Born 1957. Son of the prominent poet Ai Qing. Detained for 81 days in 2011 without charges. Lives in exile. His Sunflower Seeds installation at Tate Modern (2010) consisted of 100 million hand-painted porcelain seeds.

question: Who designed the iconic 2008 Barack Obama "Hope" poster?
context: Born 1970. Also created the OBEY Giant street-art series. The Obama poster became an iconic campaign symbol — later embroiled in a copyright dispute with the Associated Press over the source photograph.

question: Which Italian marble did Michelangelo famously prefer for sculptures like "David" and the "Pietà"?
context: From the Apuan Alps in Tuscany. Quarried since Roman times. Michelangelo personally selected his blocks from the quarries. Still commercially quarried — used in modern projects like Abu Dhabi's Sheikh Zayed Mosque.

question: The decorative style characterised by flowing, plant-inspired lines, popular around 1890–1910, is called what?
context: Roughly 1890–1910. Mucha, Klimt, Gaudí, Lalique, Tiffany. Called 'Jugendstil' in Germany, 'Stile Liberty' in Italy, 'Modernismo' in Spain — different names, same movement.

question: The bold, geometric decorative style popular in the 1920s–30s, featuring sunbursts and stepped forms, is called what?
context: Flourished in the 1920s and 30s. Chrysler Building, Empire State Building, Radio City Music Hall. Named after the 1925 Paris 'Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes'.

question: Approximately what is the size of the Mona Lisa?
answer: Roughly 77 × 53 cm (smaller than most visitors expect)
context: 77 × 53 cm — smaller than a typical poster. Most visitors find it underwhelming in person compared to its fame. Behind bulletproof glass in the Salle des États at the Louvre.

question: In which country are the prehistoric Lascaux cave paintings?
context: Around 17,000 years old. Discovered in September 1940 by four teenagers and their dog. Closed to the public since 1963 to preserve the paintings. A full-scale replica (Lascaux IV) opened in 2016.

question: The Khajuraho group of temples, famous for its sculpture, is in which country?
context: Built 950–1050 CE by the Chandela dynasty. Famous for its erotic sculptures — which actually represent only a small minority of the total artwork. UNESCO site since 1986.

question: Which Dutch city hosts the annual TEFAF art and antiques fair?
context: The European Fine Art Fair — one of the world's most prestigious art fairs. Held annually in MECC Maastricht since 1988. A major event on your own city's calendar — typically March, drawing the international art world to Limburg.

question: Who designed New York's Central Park (with Calvert Vaux), opened in the 1850s–60s?
context: 1822–1903. Considered the founder of American landscape architecture. Designed Prospect Park (Brooklyn), Stanford's campus, the US Capitol grounds, the Biltmore Estate and many more — essentially shaping how green space looks across the US.

question: The cricket Ashes are contested between which two countries?
context: Originated in 1882 after Australia defeated England and a mock obituary appeared in The Sporting Times for English cricket, declaring its ashes would be taken to Australia. A tiny urn containing burnt cricket bails is the symbolic trophy. Contested every two years.

question: What colour is the leader's jersey at the Tour de France?
context: Yellow because L'Auto (now L'Équipe), the newspaper that sponsored the Tour, was printed on yellow paper. Introduced in 1919.

question: What colour is the leader's jersey at the Giro d'Italia?
context: Pink because La Gazzetta dello Sport, the Italian sports paper that organises the Giro, is printed on pink paper. Introduced in 1931.

question: What colour is the leader's jersey at the Vuelta a España?
context: Red since 2010. The Vuelta has had several leader-jersey colours over its history (including gold and yellow in earlier editions).

question: Name the three races that make up the US Thoroughbred Triple Crown.
answer: The Kentucky Derby, the Preakness Stakes, and the Belmont Stakes
context: Kentucky Derby (first Saturday in May), Preakness Stakes (two weeks later), Belmont Stakes (about five weeks after the Derby). Only 13 horses have won all three. Secretariat (1973) still holds all three race records.
option_a: Kentucky Derby, Breeders' Cup Classic, Belmont Stakes
option_b: Kentucky Derby, Preakness Stakes, Belmont Stakes
option_c: Preakness Stakes, Travers Stakes, Belmont Stakes
option_d: Kentucky Derby, Preakness Stakes, Travers Stakes

question: What is the highest rank in professional sumo wrestling?
context: Only about 70 wrestlers have achieved this rank in 250+ years of tracking. Once a yokozuna, always a yokozuna — they can't be demoted, but are expected to retire if they underperform.

question: Which winter ice sport, involving stones and brooms, originated in Scotland?
context: Dates to 16th-century Scotland. Olympic sport since 1998. Teams of four slide granite stones down the ice toward a target ('the house'); sweepers use brooms to control the trajectory.

question: Which Dutch cyclist won the first-ever Tour de France Femmes in 2022?
context: Retired 2023. Known for powerful mountain performances. Road World Champion multiple times. Famously crashed out while leading the 2016 Rio Olympics road race — survived with fractures.

question: In which year did the Netherlands women's football team win the UEFA Women's Euro on home soil?
context: Won the Euros on home soil, beating Denmark 4–2 in the final. Vivianne Miedema became a household name. The Dutch women have maintained a top-tier international side ever since.

question: Which Dutch cyclist won the 2017 Giro d'Italia overall?
context: First Dutchman to win a Grand Tour since Joop Zoetemelk took the 1980 Tour de France. Retired from road racing in 2022 at age 31 — citing mental health as the primary reason.

question: Who broke Eddy Merckx's all-time Tour de France stage-wins record in 2024 with his 35th stage victory?
context: Broke Eddy Merckx's 34-stage record at the 2024 Tour — a record that had stood since 1975. Sprint specialist nicknamed the 'Manx Missile'.

question: How many events make up a women's heptathlon?
context: Seven events over two days: 100m hurdles, high jump, shot put, 200m, long jump, javelin, 800m. Women's equivalent to the men's decathlon (though the decathlon has ten events).

question: In which years were the Summer Olympic Games cancelled due to World War II?
context: 1940 was originally scheduled for Tokyo (moved to Helsinki after the Sino-Japanese War, then cancelled). 1944 was to be held in London. The 1916 Olympics (Berlin) had been cancelled earlier for WWI.

question: Which team won Super Bowl LX (February 2026)?
answer: The Seattle Seahawks (29–13 over the New England Patriots) — Kenneth Walker III named MVP
context: Seahawks 29, Patriots 13 at Levi's Stadium in Santa Clara. Kenneth Walker III was named MVP — an unusual MVP selection given quarterbacks normally dominate the award.

question: Which team won the 2024 NBA Finals, claiming their 18th title?
answer: The Boston Celtics (4–1 over the Dallas Mavericks)
context: 18th title, breaking the tie with the Lakers (17). Led by Jayson Tatum and Finals MVP Jaylen Brown. Swept the Mavericks 4–1.

question: Which team won the 2025 World Series, becoming the first back-to-back champions since the 1998–2000 Yankees?
answer: The Los Angeles Dodgers (defeating the Toronto Blue Jays 4–3 in a 7-game series ending with an 11-inning Game 7)
context: Defeated the Toronto Blue Jays 4–3. Game 7 went 11 innings. First back-to-back World Series champions since the Yankees' 1998–2000 three-peat. The Shohei Ohtani era.

question: Which team won the 2024 Stanley Cup, claiming their first championship?
context: Founded 1993. This was their first Cup after losing the 2023 final to the Vegas Golden Knights. Led by captain Aleksander Barkov.

question: Which two Italian cities/regions are co-hosting the 2026 Winter Olympics?
context: Italy's fourth Winter Olympics (Cortina 1956, Turin 2006, now Milano Cortina 2026). Opening 6 February 2026. First Winter Olympics split across two main venue hubs.

question: In which city is the Australian Open tennis Grand Slam held?
context: Melbourne Park since 1988. Held in January — first slam of the year. Played on hard courts.

question: In which city is Roland-Garros (the French Open) held?
context: In Paris in the Bois de Boulogne. Originally played at Stade Français before moving in 1928. Clay courts ('terre battue').

question: What does the sports abbreviation "MVP" stand for?
context: Originated in US baseball in 1911 with the Chalmers Automobile Company's MVP award. Now used across virtually all major team sports.

question: Who was the first person to run a mile in under four minutes, in May 1954?
context: 6 May 1954 at the Iffley Road track in Oxford. 3:59.4. Held the record for just 46 days before John Landy broke it. Bannister was a medical student who trained on his lunch breaks.

question: At which Olympics did rugby sevens make its Games debut?
context: Added to the Olympics for Rio 2016 (where Fiji took men's gold — their first-ever Olympic medal). Shorter, faster variant of rugby union.

question: Which Brazilian footballer, nicknamed "O Fenômeno," won the FIFA World Player of the Year three times (1996, 1997, 2002)?
answer: Ronaldo (Ronaldo Luís Nazário de Lima — not to be confused with Cristiano Ronaldo)
context: 1996, 1997 and 2002 World Player of the Year. Won the 2002 World Cup as the tournament's top scorer. Struggled with knee injuries that cost him his prime years. Not to be confused with Cristiano Ronaldo.

question: Which team sport, played 4-on-4 with mixed-gender teams and a ball you throw (never dribble) into a post basket, was invented in the Netherlands?
context: Invented in 1902 by Dutch teacher Nico Broekhuysen after he saw a similar Swedish game. Gender-mixed from the outset. International Korfball Federation (IKF) is based in Zoetermeer. Was an Olympic demonstration sport at Antwerp 1920 and Amsterdam 1928.

question: The 1054 "Great Schism" split Christianity into which two main branches?
answer: The Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church
context: Final split between Western (Rome-centred, Latin) and Eastern (Constantinople, Greek) Christianity. Mutual excommunications between Pope Leo IX and Patriarch Michael I Cerularius — only formally lifted in 1965.
option_b: The Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church

question: Name the Five Pillars of Islam.
answer: Shahada (faith), Salat (prayer), Zakat (almsgiving), Sawm (fasting during Ramadan), and Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca)
context: Shahada (profession of faith), Salat (five daily prayers), Zakat (charitable giving, ~2.5%), Sawm (Ramadan fast), Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca, once in a lifetime if able).

question: During which Islamic month do Muslims observe a dawn-to-sunset fast?
context: The ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar. A dawn-to-sunset fast for most healthy adults; breaking the fast at sunset is 'iftar'. Shifts about 11 days earlier each solar year.

question: Which religion's founder formulated the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path?
answer: Buddhism (founded by Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha)
context: Founded in the 5th–6th century BCE in India. Four Noble Truths: suffering exists, has a cause (craving), can cease, through the Eightfold Path (right view, intention, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, concentration).

question: Who are the three principal deities of the Hindu Trimurti?
context: Brahma (creator), Vishnu (preserver), Shiva (destroyer/transformer). Brahma has four faces. Vishnu has ten avatars including Rama and Krishna. Shiva is often depicted in meditation or as Nataraja, the cosmic dancer.

question: What is the Jewish "Day of Atonement" called?
context: Holiest day in Judaism. 25-hour fast of atonement. Falls 10 days after Rosh Hashanah. The 1973 Yom Kippur War between Israel and an Arab coalition broke out on this day.

question: What is the Jewish New Year called?
context: Literally 'head of the year'. Begins the Jewish religious new year. Traditional foods: apples dipped in honey (for a sweet year) and round challah.

question: Who is the chief deity in Greek mythology?
context: King of the gods. Wields thunderbolts. Numerous affairs with mortals and goddesses — the source of much Greek myth. Mountain home: Mount Olympus.

question: Who is the Roman equivalent of Zeus?
context: Roman equivalent of Zeus. Gave us Thursday: Latin 'Jovis dies' → Old English 'Þunresdæg' (Thor's day) — different etymologies for the same day in different language families.

question: Which Greek goddess represents wisdom and strategic warfare?
context: Patron of Athens. Born fully grown from Zeus's head. Associated with the owl and the olive tree. Roman equivalent: Minerva.

question: Who is the Roman god of war?
context: Roman god of war. More respected in Rome than his Greek counterpart Ares (who was often portrayed as bloodthirsty and cowardly). The red planet was named after him.

question: Which Egyptian god rules the afterlife and judgement of the dead?
answer: Osiris (with Anubis guiding souls and overseeing mummification)
context: Brother and husband of Isis. Killed by his jealous brother Set, resurrected, rules the underworld. Anubis (jackal-headed) guides souls and weighs the heart against the feather of Ma'at in judgement.

question: Who was the teacher of Plato?
context: Socrates left no writings — nearly everything known about him comes through Plato and Xenophon. Plato (c. 428–348 BCE) founded the Academy in Athens.

question: Who was the teacher of Aristotle?
context: Aristotle studied under Plato at the Academy for 20 years, then later founded the Lyceum. Also tutored the young Alexander the Great.

question: Which philosopher wrote "Cogito, ergo sum" ("I think, therefore I am")?
context: 1596–1650. Argued that the very act of doubting proves the existence of a thinker. First appeared in Latin in his 'Meditations' (1641) as 'cogito, ergo sum'.

question: Which 19th-century philosopher declared "God is dead" in "The Gay Science"?
context: 1844–1900. 'Gott ist tot' appeared in 'Die fröhliche Wissenschaft' (1882). Means that the Enlightenment has rendered belief in the Christian God intellectually unsustainable — a diagnosis of cultural change, not a celebration.

question: Who co-authored "The Communist Manifesto" with Karl Marx?
context: 1820–1895. German industrialist and philosopher. Funded Marx financially for much of his life. Co-authored the Manifesto (~12,000 words). Edited Marx's 'Das Kapital' after his death.

question: Which Chinese philosophical tradition, emphasising living in harmony with the Dao, is traditionally attributed to Laozi?
context: Laozi (possibly legendary, ~6th c BCE) is attributed with the Dao De Jing. Core concepts include wu wei (non-action) and yin–yang balance. Evolved from a philosophy into a formal religion over subsequent centuries.

question: How many animals are there in the Chinese zodiac?
context: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, Pig. Twelve-year cycle. Combined with the ten Heavenly Stems produces a 60-year cycle used in traditional Chinese dating.

question: What is the Hindu festival of lights called?
context: 'Row of lamps' in Sanskrit. A five-day festival, usually October–November. Major holiday for Hindus, Jains, Sikhs and some Buddhists. Commemorates Rama's return from exile; also a day associated with Lakshmi, goddess of prosperity.

question: What is the name of the Muslim pilgrimage to Mecca — one of the Five Pillars of Islam?
context: Five-day pilgrimage during the Islamic month of Dhu al-Hijjah. Around 2–3 million pilgrims annually. Central rituals include circling the Kaaba seven times, standing at Mount Arafat, and stoning the pillars at Mina. Every able-bodied Muslim who can afford it must perform it once in their lifetime.

question: Who is the Norse god of thunder?
context: Eldest son of Odin. Wields the hammer Mjölnir. Thursday ('Thor's day') is named after him. The Marvel film character is a significantly sanitised version of a darker and more complex mythological figure.

question: Who is the chief god of the Norse pantheon?
context: All-father. Hung from Yggdrasil (the world tree) for nine days to gain wisdom. Wednesday is 'Wōden's day' in Anglo-Saxon — the same deity under a related name.

question: Which Norse trickster god is sometimes helpful, often destructive?
context: Blood-brother of Odin. Has giant parentage. Fathered Sleipnir (the eight-legged horse), mothered Fenrir (the wolf), Jörmungandr (the serpent) and Hel (ruler of the underworld). Instrumental in Baldr's death, which triggers Ragnarök.

question: Which traditional Japanese religion venerates kami (nature spirits) and is associated with torii gates?
context: 'Way of the kami'. Animistic tradition — kami reside in mountains, rivers, trees and rocks. Torii gates mark sacred spaces. Shinto shrines (jinja) are distinct from Buddhist temples (tera). Has coexisted with Buddhism in Japan for over a thousand years.
